msg: R2/INV2 contradiction: addTodo(list, text, id) takes a caller-supplied id but the spec never requires it to reject or dedupe an id already present in 'list'. R2's stated tests do not cover a colliding id. A builder implementing R2 literally will append a duplicate-id todo, producing a list that violates INV2 ('No list produced by the module contains two todos with the same id'). INV2 is only enforced by sanitizeTodos on load (R12), not on the add path, so the invariant is unenforced/untestable for the normal add flow. Spec must state addTodo's collision semantics (reject? overwrite? generate-new is banned by R10) so the invariant holds end-to-end.

R12 (sanitizeTodos) is internally contradictory on the 'completed' field: it says drop 'any element ... whose completed is not boolean' AND in the same clause 'coerce missing/non-boolean completed to false'. These are mutually exclusive — a builder cannot both drop and coerce a non-boolean-completed element. The test ('completed coerced') implies keep+coerce, but the prose also lists non-boolean completed as a drop condition. This forces the builder to guess, and the two readings produce observably different output for input like [{id:'a',text:'x',completed:'yes'}] (kept-as-false vs dropped). Resolve to a single rule.

R12/INV1 unspecified order-of-operations lets an empty-text todo survive sanitize. R12 says sanitizeTodos requires a 'non-empty string text' AND separately 'trims text'. It does not fix the order. If the non-empty check runs before the trim (the order the prose lists them), input {id:'a',text:'   ',completed:true} passes the non-empty check (a whitespace string is non-empty), then trims to '' — yielding a todo with text:'' that violates INV1 ('text: non-empty string ... Empty-text ... never enter the list'). Spec must require trim-then-validate (drop if trimmed text is empty) so INV1 is actually enforced on load.

INV6 ('Storage write atomicity of intent: the persisted value is always the result of a serialize of the current in-memory list') is contradicted by the documented quota/private-mode failure mode (R17 note), which says when a write throws the app 'continues in-memory for the session' — i.e. localStorage now lags the in-memory list, so the persisted value is NOT the current list. INV6 as worded is both unverifiable (it asserts a property of an external store the app may fail to write) and false on the degraded path. Reword INV6 to 'each successful write reflects a complete list state (never a partial op)' and acknowledge persisted/in-memory divergence after a failed write.

INV5/R18 scope is too narrow to guarantee 'XSS via text is not possible'. R18 only mandates {todo.text} interpolation and a ban on dangerouslySetInnerHTML. But the spec leaves an unbounded id/text loaded from localStorage (R12) and gives no constraint on how the UI may render fields beyond the literal {todo.text} case — e.g. if a builder later uses todo.text in a non-escaping sink (href/src/style attribute, or todo.id rendered into the DOM), the ban on dangerouslySetInnerHTML alone does not prevent it. INV5 asserts an absolute ('not possible') that R18's grep-for-dangerouslySetInnerHTML does not actually verify. Tighten R18 to: no user-controlled value placed in any URL/attribute/style sink, and the grep/test must cover that, not just dangerouslySetInnerHTML.

R2/INV2: addTodo now MUST no-op (return input unchanged) when id already exists in list, or id is not a non-empty string, or normalized text is empty. Duplicate-id behavior is explicit and testable; INV2 updated to note addTodo rejects existing ids.

R12: removed the drop-vs-coerce contradiction for completed. Policy is now unambiguously COERCE: completed becomes true only if strictly boolean true, else false; elements are never dropped on account of completed.

R12/INV1: rewrote sanitize as an ordered 5-step pipeline that trims text THEN validates non-empty (trim-then-validate), so whitespace-only text is dropped and cannot survive into the list; INV1 preserved.

INV6: reworded from unverifiable storage-atomicity claim to best-effort persistence with in-memory state as source of truth; no longer guarantees localStorage equals memory, only no-crash-on-write-fail and never-persist-partial-op, consistent with R17 quota/private-mode degradation.

INV5/R18: dropped the absolute 'XSS impossible' overclaim; scoped guarantee to enforced sinks (React text-node escaping; no dangerouslySetInnerHTML; no todo text in url/href/src/event-handler/style sinks). R18 updated to match and verify those sinks.

spec acceptance criterion now concrete & testable: revised R2 (lines 45-53) mandates addTodo is a no-op returning a deep-equal input list when id already present, plus a named test (existing id -> same length, no new element); INV2 (lines 182-184) now enforced on the add path.

spec acceptance criterion now concrete & testable: revised R12 step 4 (lines 114-118) sets a single policy — completed becomes true only if element.completed===true, else false, element NEVER dropped on account of completed; test clause names 1/'yes'/missing -> false, kept.

spec acceptance criterion now concrete & testable: revised R12 step 3 (lines 108-113) fixes order to trim-then-validate (String(text).trim() first, drop if empty); test clause asserts whitespace-only text dropped, enforcing INV1.

PLAN §5 + §4: INV4 ('No crash on bad storage') is only half-proven. The plan assigns INV4 to storage.js loadTodos (try/catch around JSON.parse, then sanitizeTodos) AND routes the failure-mode rows 'value is not valid JSON' and 'localStorage write throws' to storage.js (§4 table). But §5 states the ENTIRE automated suite imports only '../src/todos.js' (lines 156-157, 200); storage.js is never imported or tested. So the try/catch parse-recovery path -- the literal 'localStorage value is not valid JSON -> catch parse error -> empty list' failure mode -- has zero automated coverage. sanitizeTodos tests only prove the wrong-SHAPE half (valid JSON, bad contents); they never prove the parse-THROW half, which is the actual crash risk INV4 names. A builder who forgets the try/catch (or wraps the wrong call) passes every test while INV4 is violated on real corrupt storage. The plan must either put storage.js under node:test (loadTodos is testable with an injected/stubbed storage) or down-scope INV4's claim to the logic layer.

PLAN §3 (App.jsx id gen) + §4 INV2: id generation is specified as crypto.randomUUID() 'when available, else a Date.now()-plus-counter fallback', and §4 explicitly states 'App generates fresh ids but the core is the enforcement point' for INV2. Two real gaps: (1) The fallback is demonstrably collision-prone -- two genId() calls within the same millisecond rely solely on the counter, and the plan gives NO counter-seeding/persistence rule, so after a reload the counter resets to 0 and the same-ms id 'id-<t>-0' repeats (verified: identical string produced across a simulated reload). (2) Because INV2 enforcement is 'the core' (addTodo no-ops on a duplicate id), a colliding fallback id does not corrupt the list -- it makes the Add SILENTLY DO NOTHING (no todo appended, no error). The plan never tests this end-to-end: the id path lives in untested App.jsx, the core test feeds ids directly, and no case proves 'App's generated id is actually fresh w.r.t. the loaded list.' INV2 holds as a list-property but the add can silently drop a user's todo. The plan should specify a fallback that cannot collide (e.g. monotonic counter persisted, or seed off the max existing id) and note the silent-no-op behavior.

PLAN §5 'R1/R10 (purity, static)': the purity guarantee is reduced to a source-grep asserting todos.js 'contains no window/document/localStorage, no Math.random/Date.now, no non-builtin import.' A substring grep cannot prove runtime purity and is wrong in BOTH directions (both verified): FALSE NEGATIVE -- impurity via computed/global access (globalThis['docu'+'ment'], Reflect.get(globalThis,'window'), or a dynamic import()) contains none of the banned literals and passes the grep while being impure; FALSE POSITIVE -- a banned word inside a comment or string literal ('// reads document') fails the grep though the file is pure, which can wedge the gate on a clean file. R10's stated rationale ('behavioral determinism follows from R2') is the actually-sound part; the grep adds little and gives false assurance. The plan should drop the grep to a lint advisory and instead PROVE purity behaviorally where it matters: deep-freeze inputs (already planned for INV3) plus an assertion that repeated calls with identical args yield deep-equal output (determinism). Static-grep alone should not be a verifier exit criterion for R1/R10.

PLAN §5 'INV5/INV6 (UI-layer, manual per Q4)': INV6's core safety promise -- 'when a write throws (quota/private mode) the app continues without crashing' -- is assigned to storage.js saveTodos's empty catch block (§4 table, 'localStorage write throws'). This is the single most important behavioral guarantee of INV6, yet §5 verifies it ONLY by 'manual check that (simulated) storage-disabled mode does not crash.' saveTodos is a 2-line, side-effect-only, EASILY node:test-able function (inject a storage object whose setItem throws; assert saveTodos does not throw) -- there is no DOM/React dependency and Q4's 'no component-test harness' exemption does not apply, since this is a plain JS module, not a UI component. Leaving a trivially-automatable, regression-prone safety path (a future builder removing the try/catch reintroduces a crash that no test catches) on a manual checklist is an avoidable untested seam. NOTE: distinct from F4, which ruled on INV6's WORDING; this is about the plan's TEST STRATEGY under-covering saveTodos. The plan should add a node:test for saveTodos's throw-swallowing (storage injected), not defer it to manual.

Plan §1/§3/§4/§5: moved the bad-JSON crash-recovery path out of storage.js into a pure parseStored(raw) in todos.js (try/catch JSON.parse + sanitizeTodos, null->[], never throws). storage.js loadTodos is now a one-line wrapper over getItem. Added parseStored node:test cases (null, bad JSON, non-array, valid round-trip) so INV4's crash half is proven by the automated suite.

Plan §3/§4: id generation now routes through a pure makeId(seq, rand) in todos.js (crypto.randomUUID preferred; fallback combines time + per-session counter + random suffix), making it unit-testable for format/uniqueness. Explicitly named the residual risk: a collision makes addTodo no-op and silently drops the new todo (chosen fail-safe-but-silent; louder failure out of scope).

Plan §5: demoted the brittle source-grep purity check from a verifier exit criterion to a non-blocking lint advisory. R1/R10 purity is now proven behaviorally: determinism (call twice, deep-equal output), no-mutation (frozen-input test, INV3), and import-under-node-test (fails if a browser global is touched at import).

Plan §3/§4/§5: extracted saveTodos's swallow-on-throw into a pure safeSave(writer, list) in todos.js that takes an injected writer and returns true/false, never throwing. storage.js saveTodos wraps it with real setItem. Added node:test cases injecting a non-throwing and a throwing writer, so INV6's no-crash-on-write-failure is automated, not manual.

plan acceptance criterion now concrete & testable: revised §3/§4 move INV4 crash-recovery into pure parseStored(raw) in src/todos.js (try/catch JSON.parse + sanitizeTodos, null->[], never throws); storage.js loadTodos is a one-line wrapper. §5 names dedicated node:test cases: parseStored(null)->[], parseStored('not json{')->[] (bad-JSON, no throw -- the parse-THROW half), parseStored('[]')->[], valid-list round-trip, non-array->[]. The crash/recovery path is now provable by the node:test suite.
addTodo no-ops on duplicate/invalid id (src/todos.js): isNonEmptyString(id) check + list.some(id) check before append; INV2 enforced on the add path.
sanitizeTodos step 4 coerces completed (=== true ? true : false) and never drops on completed (src/todos.js).
trim-then-validate via normalizeText before empty check in addTodo/editTodo/sanitizeTodos step 3 (src/todos.js).
Pure parseStored(raw) in src/todos.js (try/catch JSON.parse + sanitize, null/bad-JSON/non-array -> []); storage.loadTodos is a one-line wrapper. INV4 recovery now node:test-importable.
makeId(seq, rand) pure/deterministic in src/todos.js; App.jsx threads a per-session seqRef counter + crypto random suffix, prefers crypto.randomUUID. Randomness lives only in UI layer.
Pure safeSave(writer, list) in src/todos.js swallows writer throw -> false, never throws; storage.saveTodos wraps real setItem. INV6 testable via injected throwing writer.

tests/todos.test.js 'addTodo: F1 duplicate id is a NO-OP' + 'addTodo: invalid ids ... NO-OPs' + 'INV2: no two elements share an id'; mutant removing dup-guard fails these (2 fails); suite green 51/51.

tests/todos.test.js 'sanitizeTodos: F2 non-boolean completed COERCED to false and KEPT' + 'completed===true stays true' + 'truthy-but-not-true (1) does NOT become true'; mutant that drops on non-boolean completed fails these; suite green 51/51.

tests/todos.test.js 'addTodo: F3 whitespace-only NO-OP' + 'editTodo: F3 empty-after-trim no-op' + 'sanitizeTodos: F3 whitespace-only text dropped (trim-then-validate)'; mutant validating-before-trim fails these; suite green 51/51.

tests/todos.test.js 'parseStored: F6 never throws on malformed battery' + 'bad JSON -> [] (parse-throw recovery)' + null/non-array/roundtrip cases; mutant removing try/catch fails these; suite green 51/51.
tests/todos.test.js 'makeId: non-empty string' + 'deterministic given fixed (seq,rand)' + '1000 increasing-seq ids distinct' + 'differing rand distinct'; suite green 51/51.
tests/todos.test.js 'safeSave: F9 swallows throwing writer returns false' + 'calls working writer with JSON.stringify(list)'; mutant removing try/catch fails the swallow test; suite green 51/51.

package.json 'test' script is 'node --test tests/'; on Node 22 the bare dir arg resolves as a module path and crashes (MODULE_NOT_FOUND). The documented exit command 'npm test' fails. Suite itself is green via 'node --test "tests/**/*.test.js"'. Fix: quote a glob in the script.
package.json test script -> quoted glob; npm test now green on Node 22
npm test (node --test glob) green 51/51 on Node 22; broken script repaired
code attack pass: clean round, no new logic findings (F10 tooling only)

R5/R6 false premise about test structure: the spec mandates 'import { describe, it, expect } from vitest' and repeatedly refers to it()/describe(), but tests/todos.test.js uses NEITHER describe NOR it -- all 51 cases are top-level test('...', fn) from node:test (grep shows 51 'test(' and 0 'describe'/'it'). The migration must therefore map 'test' -> Vitest 'test' (or 'it'), and there are no describe blocks to preserve. The spec's import contract names 'it' as required while the real file has none; a literal reading invites either spurious wrapping in describe blocks (structural change beyond scope) or confusion. R5's import line should be expressed in terms of what the file actually uses (test/expect).

R6 mapping table is INCOMPLETE and partly mis-specified for the actual assertions in the file, risking silent coverage loss (INV-C). Actual assertion inventory: 39 deepEqual, 29 equal, 8 strictEqual, 5 ok, 3 doesNotThrow, 1 notEqual. PROBLEM 1: assert.notEqual is used at line 274 to compare two STRINGS (makeId(7,'aaa') vs makeId(7,'bbb')); R6 maps notEqual -> .not.toBe, which is correct for strings, but the spec's rationale frames notEqual only loosely. PROBLEM 2 (the real gap): R6 says 'assert.equal -> toBe for reference/primitive identity'. But assert.equal here is used BOTH for reference identity on arrays (lines 65,74,79,86,311 e.g. assert.equal(next,list)) AND for value comparison of strings (line 241 assert.equal(received, JSON.stringify(list)), line 248 '[]'). Both legitimately map to toBe since node:assert/strict equal is === (verified: it throws on distinct equal arrays). The hazard: a translator following R6 might use toEqual for the string-value cases, which is still correct, but for the array-identity cases toEqual would be a SILENT downgrade. The spec flags the no-op array cases but does NOT enumerate which specific lines are reference-identity assert.equal vs value assert.equal -- leaving the most dangerous translation (line 65/74/79/86/311) to translator judgment with no checklist.

Assertion-message preservation is unspecified, and the file relies heavily on per-iteration failure messages. Many assertions pass a 3rd-arg message (e.g. line 65 'duplicate-id add returns the input list unchanged', line 86 id=${...} must be a no-op, line 112/113 per-value messages, line 188/189 parseStored(${raw}) threw/must return array). In node:assert these messages are the diagnostic shown on failure inside loop-based parameterized cases (R6 'Loop-based parameterized assertions retain every iteration'). Vitest's expect(a).toBe(b) takes NO message argument; the per-iteration context must be moved to it.each / test.each names or a wrapping message, otherwise a failing iteration becomes undiagnosable. The spec's R6 requires retaining every iteration but says nothing about preserving these failure messages -- a loss of diagnostic fidelity that R6's falsifier ('fewer passing assertions') would not catch.

SessionStart hook migration (R7.3) will SILENTLY no-op if Bun is absent, unlike CI (R7.2 adds a setup-bun step). Current .claude/settings.json:31 runs 'npm install --no-audit --no-fund || true' -- the '|| true' swallows all failures. R7.3 changes it to 'bun install' but the spec gives NO provisioning requirement for the local/session environment equivalent to R7.2's setup-bun for CI. If a contributor's Claude Code session lacks bun on PATH, 'bun install || true' succeeds with no node_modules installed, and the failure is invisible (the whole point of the hook -- auto-installing deps -- silently breaks). The spec asserts deps get installed at session start but provides no falsifier that bun is actually reachable in that context. At minimum the spec must state the Bun availability assumption for the SessionStart environment or the hook must not swallow a missing-bun error.

Test-glob portability between node:test and Vitest is unverified. package.json test script is currently: node --test "tests/**/*.test.js". R4a requires the new script to be 'vitest run'. Vitest's default include is '**/*.{test,spec}.?(c|m)[jt]s?(x)' which covers tests/todos.test.js, so a bare 'vitest run' works -- BUT if the migrator copies the explicit glob (vitest run tests/**/*.test.js) the shell-vs-vitest glob semantics differ and ** behavior is not identical. The spec does not state whether to keep or drop the explicit path argument, nor confirm the single test file is actually collected by Vitest's default include. Recommend the spec pin the exact new test script body and assert Vitest collects tests/todos.test.js.

Vitest brings a peer/runtime dependency that N3 may forbid. R4/N3 say 'no version upgrades except adding Vitest (and a DOM env only if R5 proves needed)'. But Vitest depends on a specific Vite major. The repo pins vite ^5.4.0; modern Vitest (1.6+/2.x/3.x on Bun 1.3.11 era) may require/peer-depend on a Vite version that conflicts with ^5.4.0, forcing either a Vitest version old enough for Vite 5 or a Vite bump that N3 prohibits. The spec never pins a Vitest version nor checks Vitest<->Vite peer compatibility against the pinned vite ^5.4.0. This can make R1 (clean bun install succeeds) fail on peer-dependency resolution or force an out-of-scope Vite upgrade. Spec should pin a Vitest version known-compatible with Vite 5.

R7.2 falsifier is too weak / partly wrong for the CI workflow. The falsifier is: grep -rn 'npm ' --include=<files> still matches an operative command. But .github/workflows/sdlc-arbiter-gate.yml lines 87-88 are 'npm install --no-audit --no-fund' and 'npm test'. After migration to 'bun install' + 'bun run test', the grep for 'npm ' would pass -- yet the workflow may STILL be broken if the setup-bun step is omitted or placed after the install step. The grep-based falsifier cannot detect ordering (Bun must be set up BEFORE bun install). R7.2 text requires the setup step but the FALSIFIER does not verify it exists or is ordered first. Also: --no-audit/--no-fund are npm-only flags; the spec says 'Bun equivalents' but bun install has no such flags, so a literal port that appends them errors. Spec should specify the exact bun install invocation (no npm-only flags) and a falsifier that checks setup-bun precedes the install.

R3 mandates 'bun install produces a working node_modules' and R1's clean-install path removes node_modules, but the spec never addresses 'bun install --frozen-lockfile' for CI reproducibility (INV-E). Without --frozen-lockfile, CI's bun install can silently update the committed lockfile if package.json drifts, undermining INV-E (reproducible/authoritative lockfile). Recommend the CI install use bun install --frozen-lockfile so a lockfile mismatch fails CI rather than silently re-resolving.

README line 14 ('npm test # node:test suite (51 tests)') describes the runner as node:test in prose, and line 17 'Requires Node 20+'. R7.5 covers revising these to Bun, but the spec does not say whether to KEEP the literal '51 tests' count claim. If the migrated suite restructures cases (e.g. test.each collapsing loop iterations into a single test() with multiple expects), the visible Vitest test count may differ from 51 even though assertion coverage is preserved (R6/N2). The spec's success metric '51-case test suite' and README's '51 tests' could become inaccurate; spec should clarify whether 51 refers to test() blocks or assertions and keep the README consistent.

Resolved. Spec section 1/2 and R5 now state the file has exactly 51 top-level test() blocks and zero describe/it (verified: grep shows 51 test(, 0 describe, 0 it). The import contract is corrected to exactly { test, expect } from vitest -- 'it' is no longer named as required -- and N2/R5 explicitly forbid introducing describe/it wrapping. The false premise the finding cited is gone.

Resolved. R6 adds a 'Critical assert.equal disambiguation (F12)' block that enumerates the dangerous reference-identity array cases (lines 65,74,79,86,311) which MUST stay toBe, separately from the string-value cases (241,248) and the scalar cases, and the falsifier proves it with a known-broken core returning a copy on a no-op. The specific checklist the finding asked for is now present.

Resolved. New R6a mandates preservation of per-iteration diagnostic messages, enumerates the message-bearing lines (64,65,86,112,113,176,188,189,270,311,470,471), requires moving them to test.each names or an equivalent, forbids dropping a message with no replacement, and adds a falsifier that the failing-iteration identifier must survive against a broken core. INV-C now lists diagnostic-message loss as coverage loss.

Resolved. New R7a forbids the naive '... && bun install || true' for SessionStart: it requires a command -v bun check, a loud stderr diagnostic + non-success signal when Bun is absent, restricts '|| true' to the benign no-package.json case, and Q4 records the Bun-availability decision. The silent no-op the finding flagged is now explicitly prohibited with a falsifier.

Resolved (minor). R4a now pins the test script to exactly 'vitest run' with no glob argument and asserts Vitest's default include pattern collects tests/todos.test.js; the falsifier covers the case where a glob had to be added without updating the spec. The keep-or-drop ambiguity is closed.

Resolved (minor). R4 and N3 now pin vitest@^1.6 (the 1.x line compatible with vite ^5.4.0) and forbid pulling 2.x/3.x; the falsifier fails if the installed Vitest major is outside ^1.6 or its peer-deps conflict with vite ^5.4.0. The peer-compat gap and out-of-scope Vite bump risk are addressed.

Resolved (minor). R7.2 now pins the exact CI commands to 'bun install --frozen-lockfile' then 'bun run test', requires an oven-sh/setup-bun step BEFORE the install/test step, drops the npm-only --no-audit/--no-fund flags, and the falsifier explicitly rejects those flags surviving. The ordering and npm-only-flag hazards are covered.
Resolved (nit). R7.2 and INV-E now mandate 'bun install --frozen-lockfile' in CI so a stale lockfile fails CI rather than silently re-resolving, satisfying the reproducibility concern.

Resolved (nit). New R7b clarifies that '51' refers to test() blocks (not assertions), requires README/added comments to state '51 test() blocks' and not a stale node --test runner, with a falsifier on conflating blocks with assertions.

Silent coverage loss in test.each conversions (plan Step 3.D, lines 219 and 223). Vitest/Jest test.each spreads any ELEMENT that is itself an array as the argument row. The invalid-ids array ['', 42, null, undefined, {}, [], true] (line 219) and the completed-coercion array [1, 'yes', 0, '', null, undefined, {}, []] (line 223) each contain a bare [] element. Because Array.isArray([]) is true, test.each does NOT wrap it; it spreads [] into ZERO arguments, so the callback receives badId/val === undefined for that case instead of []. Result: the original src test cases addTodo(list,'text',[]) (line 84 loop) and sanitizeTodos completed:[] (line 110 loop) are silently dropped and replaced by a duplicate of the undefined case. This is exactly the INV-C/R6 'every iteration retained' failure the spec is built to prevent: the migrated suite goes green with weaker coverage. Fix: wrap each row, e.g. test.each([[''],[42],[null],[undefined],[{}],[[]],[true]]) so [] is passed as the single arg, or use test.each over [v] tuples.

Contradictory/wrong test.each example at plan line 218-219. The first form given for the invalid-ids loop is test.each([['', 42, null, undefined, {}, [], true].map(v => [v])]) . This evaluates to test.each([ [[''],[42],[null],...] ]) -- an OUTER array of length 1 whose single element is the 7-element table. test.each iterates the outer array, so it runs ONE case and passes the whole nested table as the callback's first arg, collapsing all 7 invalid-id cases into one. The plan then writes 'i.e.' and gives a different (flat) form, so the same step presents two non-equivalent expressions. A builder copying the first literally drops 6 of 7 cases. The intended wrap is test.each(['', 42, null, undefined, {}, [], true].map(v => [v])) (no extra outer [...] brackets).

Unverified %p format specifier in test.each titles (plan 3.D lines 219, 223, 225, 228 and note at 221). The plan relies on %p ('pretty-prints the case value') to carry the dropped per-iteration diagnostic required by R6a. Vitest 1.6's own API docs only enumerate %i, %s, %f for test.each name interpolation; %p is a Jest pretty-format specifier whose support in Vitest 1.x is not documented/guaranteed. If %p is not interpolated, the title renders the literal 'id=%p' and the failing-iteration diagnostic R6a mandates is NOT surfaced -- the exact R6a falsifier. Recommend %s (documented) or hmtBcproperty/template-literal naming, and have the verifier assert the rendered case name actually contains the value.

Resolved. Both affected loops now wrap every row fully: invalid-ids is test.each([[''],[42],[null],[undefined],[{}],[[]],[true]]) (plan line 239) and completed-coercion is test.each([[1],['yes'],[0],[''],[null],[undefined],[{}],[[]]]) (plan line 258). The bare [] element is now [[]] in both, so it is passed as one argument instead of spreading to zero. The CRITICAL rule note at lines 215-225 documents the Array.isArray spread hazard and mandates literal per-row wrapping. The 7-case and 8-case counts are reasserted (lines 249, 268). The INV-C silent-coverage-loss path the blocker identified is closed.

Resolved. The contradictory first form test.each([[...].map(v => [v])]) with the extra outer brackets is removed; only the canonical literally-wrapped rows remain. The single surviving .map(v => [v]) mention (line 224) is an explicit PROHIBITION ('Do NOT use the .map(v => [v]) shorthand — write the wrapped rows out literally'), not an example a builder could copy. No two non-equivalent expressions are presented for the same step. The one-case-collapse hazard is eliminated.

Resolved. Operative test.each titles now use %s (and %#/%i/%f per the Title-token rule at lines 227-233); the sole surviving %p (line 228) is inside that rule's 'NOT guaranteed in the 1.x line' warning, not in any title. The plan also instructs function-form titles with JSON.stringify for object/array rows where %s renders poorly, and Step 10 item 4 has the verifier assert the rendered case name surfaces the failing value (R6a). The undocumented-specifier diagnostic-loss risk is closed.

Test-count contradiction makes R7/§6.3 falsifiable against the wrong number. Spec asserts '51 pure-core test blocks' (R7, §6.3, §0) and R7's *falsify* condition is 'bun run test changes count/result'. But 'bun run test' reports 87 tests, not 51: there are 51 top-level test()/test.each() statements, and test.each expands (e.g. the addTodo bad-id table = 7 cases, the sanitize/parseStored tables more) to 87 total. A verifier checking the count printed by 'bun run test' sees 87. The spec conflates 'number of test statements' (51) with 'number of tests bun reports' (87). Pin R7 to one unambiguous, observable number (recommend: 87 tests passing) or it cannot be checked as written.

R6 mandates deleting the ONLY visual selected-filter cue with no requirement to recreate it. src/index.css line 81 '.filters button[aria-pressed=true]{font-weight:bold;text-decoration:underline}' is today the sole *visual* indication of which filter is active. R6 explicitly lists this aria-pressed selector among rules to REMOVE. A5 only requires the *programmatic* state (aria-pressed) be preserved, not a visual indicator. So a migration can satisfy both R6 and A5 while leaving the active filter visually indistinguishable from inactive ones (a sighted-user regression). INV-2 forbids trading an affordance for a prettier look, but the visual selected-state is not enumerated as an affordance to preserve. Add an explicit requirement that the active filter remain visually distinguished (and that this distinction itself meet A1/A6 not-color-alone).

Q4 and Q5 are left as 'proposed defaults' but each gates whether the §4 bar is testable/holdable, so they are unsafe to defer into planning unresolved. Q4 decides the entire a11y verification mechanism: with no jsdom and no DOM tests today (confirmed: tests/ has only todos.test.js, node env), §6 criterion 5 ('a manual/automated a11y check confirms A1-A7') has no defined runner — A2/A3 (focus ring, keyboard Enter/Escape) are essentially unverifiable without the render harness, yet the harness is only a 'fallback-able' default. Q5 (native vs Radix checkbox) determines whether A4 ('checkboxes stay native input or equivalent role') and the App.jsx checked/onChange contract survive; Risk-5 notes Radix renders button role=checkbox. These two open questions should be resolved at the spec gate, not carried into the plan as defaults, since acceptance §6.5/§6.3 shape depends on them.

A1 large-text threshold is factually wrong. A1 defines large text as '>= 24px, or >= 19px bold'. WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.3 defines large text as 18pt (24px) regular OR 14pt (18.66px ~ 18.67px) bold — i.e. ~18.7px bold, not 19px. Text at 18.7-19px bold would be wrongly held to the 4.5:1 normal-text bar instead of 3:1. Minor since this UI uses ~16px/1.5rem text (all subject to 4.5:1 anyway), but the bar should state the correct WCAG threshold to be a valid contrast check.

A3 omits the onBlur-commit edit behavior that exists today and could regress. App.jsx line 132 wires onBlur={() => commitEdit(todo.id)} on the edit input — blurring the field COMMITS the edit. A3 enumerates 'commit on Enter and cancel on Escape' as the exact behavior that MUST survive, but never mentions blur-commit. If the Radix/shadcn input or a focus-management change alters blur timing (e.g. autoFocus interplay, or a wrapping component stealing focus), the blur-commit could silently break or double-fire commitEdit, and no listed falsify catches it. Add blur-commit to the A3/behavior-preservation list.

Acceptance criterion §6.4 ('no element relying on removed bespoke classes') is partially unfalsifiable because one used class has NO rule today. App.jsx applies className='count' (line 159) but src/index.css defines NO '.count' rule (confirmed). The spec's R6/§6 enumerate the classes to remove (.app/.add-form/.todo-list/.todo-item/.text.completed/.footer/.filters/aria-pressed) but omit '.count'. A verifier auditing 'old bespoke rules are gone' against the enumerated list will pass even though .count is an unstyled class in the markup. Minor, but the migration's class inventory in R6 is incomplete; either drop the .count className or list it.

Autoprefixer is named in acceptance/risks but never required, leaving a pinning gap R2 cannot falsify. §6.1 and R2 reference 'PostCSS/autoprefixer peers' and 'autoprefixer' as if part of the deliverable, but no requirement (R1/R2) actually mandates autoprefixer be installed/configured — and Tailwind v4 drops the need for a separate autoprefixer/postcss-import setup entirely (relevant to Q2). So depending on the v3-vs-v4 choice, 'autoprefixer appears in package.json' is either required or wrong, yet §6.1's wording implies it must appear. Reconcile the autoprefixer expectation with the Q2 Tailwind-major decision so §6.1 is not a false positive/negative against a valid v4 setup.

Plan's own mandated contrast harness FAILS on the plan's own pinned border token. §4 token table (plan line 171) asserts --border #e2e8f0 (214.3 31.8% 91.4%) meets '>=3:1 as UI boundary (A1 1.4.11)'. Actual computed WCAG ratio of #e2e8f0 on #ffffff is 1.23:1 (verified via relative-luminance calc), not >=3:1. §8 line 288 REQUIRES the a11y harness to compute the WCAG ratio for the 'border/bg' pair and assert each pair '>= its threshold (4.5 / 3.0)'. At 1.23:1 the border/bg assertion at 3.0 will FAIL, so a builder implementing the plan verbatim produces a harness that cannot pass with the pinned Slate tokens. Either the plan's 3:1 border claim is false (border is decorative, not a required 1.4.11 boundary -> then §8 must NOT assert border/bg at 3.0), or the token must change. As written the plan is internally contradictory and un-buildable. Fix: drop border/bg from the §8 3:1 assertion (borders here are not the sole means of identifying a control, so 1.4.11 does not apply) and correct the §4 table's false '>=3:1' claim.

Render harness (§8) omits RTL cleanup between renders -> single-element assertions will throw. The project runs 'vitest run' with NO config file; the plan adds none (no vitest.config, no globals:true, no setupFiles). @testing-library/react's auto-cleanup ONLY registers an afterEach when a global afterEach exists (i.e. Vitest globals:true) — with Vitest's default globals:false and no manual afterEach(cleanup), rendered DOM accumulates across test() blocks in the shared happy-dom document. §8 has the harness render <App/> in many separate cases (A4 heading, toggle, edit x3, filters, focus, contrast). After the first render, getByRole('heading',{level:1}) (which asserts EXACTLY ONE h1) and other getByRole/getByLabelText single-element queries throw 'found multiple elements', failing the A4/A5 assertions the plan relies on. The plan must specify either globals:true (via a vitest.config, which then risks R7's node-run bookkeeping) OR an explicit afterEach(cleanup) import in the a11y file. As written §8 is not runnable green.

Delete-button 'variant=destructive' escape hatch (§7) can ship a AA-failing token the harness won't catch. §7 (Delete row) says: 'shadcn <Button variant=ghost size=sm>Delete</Button> (or variant=destructive if contrast holds; default ghost keeps it neutral).' shadcn Slate's destructive-foreground (#f8fafc) on destructive (#ef4444) computes to 3.6:1 — BELOW AA 4.5:1 for normal text (verified). The plan hedges 'if contrast holds' but (a) provides no ratio and no gate, and (b) §8's contrast assertion only covers the §4 table pairs (bg/fg, primary/primary-fg, muted-fg/bg, border/bg, ring/bg) — destructive/destructive-foreground is NOT among them. So a builder choosing variant=destructive ships a 3.6:1 Delete label that A1/INV-3 forbids, and the mandated harness stays green (false pass). Fix: either forbid variant=destructive for the Delete text button, or add destructive/destructive-fg to the §4 pinned table AND the §8 assertion so the 3.6:1 failure is caught.

§8 contrast assertion tests hardcoded hex constants copied from §4, not the tokens actually shipped in index.css -> it cannot catch INV-3 drift. §8 line 288: 'Uses the hex values from section 4 ... a ~15-line relative-luminance helper inside the test file.' The harness re-declares the same hex literals the plan lists; it never parses src/index.css or reads the computed CSS custom properties. So if the builder authors the :root tokens as HSL (shadcn's cssVariables:true ships hsl() triplets, e.g. --muted-foreground:215.4 16.3% 46.9%) and a value drifts from §4, or the builder pastes a lighter shadcn 'default'-style muted, the test still passes because it grades its own copy of the numbers, not the stylesheet. The spec's INV-3 ('theme tokens MUST NOT ship a fg/bg pair below AA') and A1 are about the SHIPPED tokens; a self-referential constant table is a tautology. Given muted-foreground sits at only 4.76:1 (0.26 above the 4.5 line), this blind spot matters. Fix: derive the asserted values from the actual index.css :root block (parse the hsl vars / getComputedStyle) so the check binds to what renders.

§8 A2 focus-ring 'assertion' cannot actually verify a VISIBLE focus indicator in happy-dom -> A2 falsifier ('tabbing produces no visible focus change') stays unprovable. §8 line 287 proves A2 by (a) element.focus() -> toHaveFocus() and (b) 'assert focus-visible ring class present on primitives'. happy-dom does not do layout/paint and does not resolve Tailwind utility classes to computed styles, nor evaluate :focus-visible; so the test can only assert the shadcn className STRING contains 'focus-visible:ring-...'. That proves the class is written, not that a ring is rendered with >=3:1 contrast (A2's actual bar). If shadcn's ring token or ring-offset is overridden, or a utility is purged, the class-string test still passes. This makes A2 the weakest-verified bar: the plan should either state A2 is class-presence-only (a documented limitation) plus a manual keyboard check, or note that focus-ring contrast (ring vs adjacent, A2 >=3:1) is not machine-verified by this harness. As written §8 overclaims A2 coverage.

Plan's runtime dependency list omits @radix-ui/react-slot, which shadcn's stock button imports -> build break on the hand-create path. shadcn's default button.jsx begins 'import { Slot } from "@radix-ui/react-slot"' (the asChild pattern). Plan line 60 lists runtime deps as ONLY class-variance-authority, clsx, tailwind-merge, @radix-ui/react-checkbox, and line 86 explicitly asserts 'No @radix-ui/react-* beyond react-checkbox — button/input are plain shadcn (no Radix dep)'. That claim is FALSE for the stock button. §5 permits 'hand-create matching the stock source if the CLI misbehaves under Bun'; on that path nothing installs react-slot, so @/components/ui/button.jsx's 'import { Slot }' is unresolvable and 'bun run build' fails (R3 falsifier: shadcn import fails to resolve; INV-4). Even on the CLI path, 'bunx shadcn add button' auto-adds @radix-ui/react-slot, which then (i) contradicts the plan's exact-pin policy (react-slot not in the enumerated pin set, line 92) and (ii) appears in package.json/bun.lock un-pinned, and (iii) is a @radix-ui/react-* the plan swore wouldn't exist (R2/INV-4 'no dual/loose pinning'). Fix: add @radix-ui/react-slot@1.x to the runtime deps + exact-pin list and correct the 'no Radix dep for button' claim.

Visual card regression (R6/G4): original index.css had body{background:#f5f5f5} (gray page) with .app{background:#fff} (white card) — a visible card-on-page separation. New code renders body as bg-background (#ffffff) AND the App wrapper as bg-card (#ffffff, src/App.jsx:108) — both pure white — so the card no longer visually stands out from the page; only a faint shadow-sm remains. The 'card look preserved' claim in plan §7 does not hold: with identical bg the card is effectively invisible. A5 filter-cue test is class-string-coupled, not behavior-coupled (tests/App.a11y.test.jsx:139-144): it asserts the active filter's className matches /bg-primary/ and inactive does not. This proves the JSX applies variant='default' vs 'ghost', but would still pass if the distinction were, e.g., color-only in a future change (bg-primary vs bg-secondary both fill), since it only checks for the literal 'bg-primary' token — it does not assert the *non-color-alone* property A5/A6 actually require. Disclosed in the plan as class-assertion, but flagging as a weak (tautology-adjacent) check that couples to an implementation string rather than the a11y invariant.
