Valid minor presentation regression, confirmed and fixed. Original UI had gray page (#f5f5f5) vs white card; migration made both pure white, collapsing the card separation. Fix: src/index.css --background now 210 40% 98% (light Slate gray, ~#f8fafc) while --card stays 0 0% 100% (white), restoring visible card-on-page separation. body is bg-background, App wrapper bg-card (App.jsx:108). Distinction is genuine and the comment documents the AA guard (muted-fg/background stays 4.54:1 at this L). Fixed + proven; verdict accepted.

Valid nit: original filter-cue test coupled to the bg-primary color token only, so a future color-only-vs-color regression would pass while violating A5/A6 not-color-alone. Fix: tests/App.a11y.test.jsx:144-147,152-154 now additionally asserts font-semibold and underline (a non-color distinguishing feature) present on the active filter and absent on inactive ones, and that the cue moves with selection. App.jsx:192-212 applies 'font-semibold underline underline-offset-4' to the active filter. The check now binds to the non-color a11y invariant, not just an implementation color string. Fixed + proven; verdict accepted.

DATA LOSS on load failure/timeout. The failure table (§9) says an unreachable server on load makes loadTodos() resolve to []. But App.jsx:58-60 saves the whole list on EVERY todos change via useEffect(() => saveTodos(todos),[todos]). Sequence: server slow/down at mount -> load yields [] -> setTodos([]) -> save effect fires -> PUT [] -> the SQLite file is WIPED. This is a NEW failure class localStorage never had (its load was synchronous and never 'failed-to-empty then overwrote persisted bytes'). The spec's INV-F claims best-effort save 'preserves' localStorage behavior, but here a transient server hiccup destroys durable data. Spec must forbid the save effect from firing until a successful load completes (or distinguish 'load failed' from 'genuinely empty'). [Renumbered from F23 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. Hydration guard (D2 steps 1-3), INV-H, T8: save effect no-ops until GET settles; no PUT [] on mount.

Mount-time PUT [] overwrites real data even when the server is HEALTHY. D2 mandates 'start empty ([]), then load async and populate.' But the existing save effect (App.jsx:58) fires on the initial [] state BEFORE the GET resolves. Ordering on the wire is not guaranteed: the mount-time 'PUT []' can be received by the server AFTER the GET returns the real list but is not yet applied client-side, or can simply race a save from another tab. Result: last-write-wins picks 'PUT []' and clears persisted todos. The spec's 'single writer, last-write-wins' story (§8) is INCONSISTENT with React effect timing: the client emits a save of stale/empty state it never intended to persist. Spec must specify a 'hydrated' guard so no save is emitted until the initial load has populated state. [Renumbered from F24 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. D2 step 3 forbids PUT for [] and for the load-applied value; first PUT = genuine user edit; INV-H/T8.

Q4 transient IS a user-visible behavior change for RETURNING users, and the spec's dismissal is unfounded. §D2/Q4 argue the empty-list flash is 'not a functionality change' because 'the app already renders an empty list for a brand-new user' and localhost is 'effectively instantaneous.' But today loadTodos() is SYNCHRONOUS (App.jsx:49 useState(() => loadTodos())) so a returning user with N todos sees their list on the FIRST paint, never an empty frame. After D2 they see empty-then-populated on every page load. That flash-of-empty-content is a new, observable state that violates INV-B ('every interaction's result... the same as today') for the load interaction. 'localhost is instantaneous' is an unproven assumption: cold server start, DB file creation on first run, GC pause, or a busy machine make it a visible flicker (and the count reads '0 items left' mid-flash). No timeout/latency budget is specified to bound it. [Renumbered from F25 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. Empty-flash acknowledged as a real returning-user change; bounded (<=200ms budget, 3000ms hard timeout), no new UI; escalated as Q4.

No fetch timeout or rejection-handling contract is specified; unhandled promise rejection risk. §D2 says loadTodos/saveTodos become fetch-based Promises, and the failure table says a failed load 'resolves to [] (or rejects -> app treats as empty)'. That parenthetical 'or rejects' is a real gap: fetch() only rejects on network error, NOT on HTTP 500/4xx (those resolve with ok=false), and response.json() throws on a non-JSON/HTML error body. If storage.js does not explicitly catch both fetch rejection AND json() parse throw, the returned Promise rejects; there is no .catch in App.jsx's load path (D2 just does setState on resolve), producing an unhandled promise rejection and a blank app. Also NO timeout is specified: a server that accepts the socket but never responds hangs loadTodos() forever -> the app is stuck on the empty frame indefinitely, with the save effect having already PUT []. Spec must fix: explicit catch mapping every load failure (network, non-2xx, bad JSON, timeout) to a defined outcome, and a bounded timeout. [Renumbered from F26 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. §6 contract table maps every failure (non-2xx, network, json-throw, 3000ms timeout) to a fallback; mandates response.ok + try/catch; INV-F/T5.

Save-ordering race: an in-flight PUT from stale state can overwrite a newer PUT (out-of-order completion). The save effect (App.jsx:58) fires a new PUT on every keystroke-driven todos change (add/toggle/edit). fetch does NOT guarantee response/processing order: PUT(v1) and PUT(v2) issued back-to-back can arrive/commit at the server in either order (connection reuse, retries, TCP). §8's 'last-write-wins' is defined by WALL-CLOCK arrival, but the client's own two saves can arrive out of send-order, so the DB can end up holding v1 (older) after v2 (newer) was already sent -> silent data regression on the NEXT load. localStorage.setItem was synchronous and strictly ordered, so this class did not exist. Spec's concurrency section only reasons about 'two tabs'; it misses same-client rapid sequential saves. Needs a request-sequencing/coalescing requirement (e.g. abort in-flight save, or monotonic sequence guard server-side). [Renumbered from F27 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. §8 serialize/coalesce-or-sequence-guard; INV-J/T10 (v2 wins over delayed v1).

Contradiction: §8 'no server-side validation of list semantics' vs Form-A schema constraints (§5) with no defined error path. Form A declares id TEXT PRIMARY KEY non-empty, text NOT NULL non-empty(post-trim), completed 0/1. The client is trusted to have sanitized, but sanitizeTodos runs on LOAD, not before every save; App.jsx sends whatever is in state. A PUT can legitimately carry data that violates schema: (a) duplicate ids are impossible from the core, but a JSON body with two identical ids would hit the PRIMARY KEY constraint and the INSERT THROWS mid-transaction; (b) text that is a valid non-empty JS string of only characters that... actually the client trims, but the server does not, so a client-sent ' ' (space) is non-empty at the DB layer yet violates the 'non-empty post-trim' invariant the schema claims to enforce. The spec never defines what the API returns when a constraint fires (500? partial? the atomic rollback leaves the OLD list but saveTodos must still resolve false, not reject). Undefined -> plan will guess. Needs an explicit constraint-violation -> 4xx/false, rollback, no-crash contract. [Renumbered from F28 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. §8 defines constraint-violation -> rollback -> false, no crash (INV-K).

Existing dev/build/preview tooling breaks or silently no-ops persistence, unaddressed. package.json scripts today are pure Vite: dev=vite, build=vite build, preview=vite preview (frontend-only). §D3 says 'in build/preview the same server serves the API and the built static assets alongside it (single origin)' but 'vite preview' serves ONLY static assets on a Vite port and knows nothing about the new server; a relative fetch('/api/todos') from a vite-preview origin will 404 (no proxy in preview; Vite proxy config only applies to dev). So after this change, 'bun run preview' yields an app whose loads/saves silently fail (INV-F swallows it) -> the app appears to work but persists NOTHING, and per F23 may even PUT [] if it 404s to a real server. The spec defers exact scripts to the plan but does NOT state that the current 'vite preview' path must be replaced/removed; leaving it means a documented command produces a broken-persistence app. Also unspecified: does 'bun run dev' now require the server to be started separately (two processes) or does one script launch both? Concurrency of process startup (server not up when browser first GETs) directly triggers F23/F25. [Renumbered from F29 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. API reachability in dev + preview + build made a hard requirement (INV-I/T9).

INV-G is under-specified and not cleanly testable as stated. T7 promises a 'static/config check that the SQLite file path is in .gitignore and the server binds to loopback', but the spec fixes neither (a) the exact gitignore pattern nor (b) the exact bind address. Current .gitignore has no data/ entry (verified); Q6 leaves the path open (data/todos.sqlite is only an example). A static test cannot assert 'the SQLite path is git-ignored' without a fixed path/pattern -> the plan could put the DB at repo root and forget the ignore rule, silently committing user data. 'localhost' is also ambiguous: binding to 0.0.0.0 also answers on localhost but is NOT loopback-only (exposes the API to the LAN, violating N5). Spec should pin the bind address literally to 127.0.0.1 (not 'localhost', which can resolve to ::1 or be intercepted) and pin the exact gitignore line, so INV-G's test is falsifiable rather than aspirational. [Renumbered from F30 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. INV-G pinned to literal 127.0.0.1 bind + literal .gitignore line.

Missing CSRF/local-origin trust and unbounded request-body considerations for the new write endpoint. The spec adds a state-mutating PUT/POST /api/todos bound to localhost with NO auth (N5) and no origin/content-type check. Any web page the user visits while the server runs can issue a cross-origin POST to http://127.0.0.1:PORT/api/todos (a classic 'localhost CSRF' / DNS-rebinding target) and silently overwrite or wipe the user's todo file, since there is no same-origin enforcement, no CSRF token, and CORS does not block simple POSTs from being SENT. localStorage was origin-scoped and unreachable this way, so this is a NEW attack surface the 'no functionality change' framing hides. Also no max-body-size is specified: a whole-list PUT with a giant JSON body could OOM the server. Spec should require an Origin/Host allowlist check (reject non-localhost Origin) and a body-size cap, or explicitly accept the risk for a local demo with rationale. [Renumbered from F31 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. Origin/Host check + body-size cap (INV-L).

T4 (atomic-replace proof) as scoped by INV-D under-specifies the Form-A failure that actually breaks atomicity: a duplicate-id INSERT. §8 says 'Form A: wrap delete+insert in a single transaction' guarantees the DB holds either old or new whole list. True for a crash, but a PRIMARY KEY collision within the new list makes the second INSERT throw AFTER the DELETE has removed the old rows; if the server does not wrap in an explicit transaction with rollback (just relies on autocommit-per-statement), the DELETE commits and the failed INSERT leaves the DB EMPTY (old list gone, new list not written) -> exactly the 'mix/partial' state INV-D forbids, and worse, total loss. The spec asserts atomicity as a property of 'a transaction' but does not require the plan to ROLLBACK on ANY error mid-PUT (not just crash). T4 as written only simulates 'interrupted/mid-transaction failure' generically; it should explicitly include a constraint-violation-triggered rollback case so the verifier proves the DELETE is undone. [Renumbered from F32 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. Explicit transaction rolled back on ANY error (autocommit forbidden); T4 adds duplicate-id rollback case.

React 18 StrictMode double-mount in dev multiplies the mount-time save/load and can persist a spurious empty write. Vite React apps commonly wrap the tree in <React.StrictMode>, which in dev intentionally mounts, unmounts, and re-mounts the component, invoking effects twice. Combined with D2's load-on-mount + the always-on save effect (App.jsx:58), StrictMode fires: mount1 load + save([]) , unmount, mount2 load + save([]). Two overlapping GETs and two PUT [] hit the server; the load Promise from the first (unmounted) instance may resolve and setState on a stale/dead component (React warns) or its result is discarded while its save([]) already landed. The spec never mentions StrictMode and its 'single writer' reasoning assumes one mount. Since dev is a primary run mode (bun run dev, Q2), the plan needs an explicit requirement: guard the effect against double-invoke (abortable load, no save until hydrated) and specify whether StrictMode is on. Otherwise F23/F24 reproduce reliably in dev, not just under adverse timing. [Renumbered from F33 due to a cross-branch ref collision with ISSUE-7's identically-numbered finding; see PR #16.]
Resolved. Abortable load + guarded save robust to StrictMode double-mount; T8 repeats under double-mount.

PLAN §3.1 + §6-step-3 will DELETE the existing '@' path alias and break the whole build. The live vite.config.js (verified) contains a resolve.alias block mapping '@' -> ./src, which App.jsx and every shadcn component depend on (imports '@/lib/utils', '@/components/ui/button', '@/components/ui/input', '@/components/ui/checkbox' at App.jsx:23-26). The plan's §3.1 shows a full defineConfig({ plugins:[react()], server:{proxy:...} }) with NO resolve.alias, and §6 step 3 instructs the builder to 'apply §3 verbatim'. Following that literally removes the alias -> Vite dev/build fails to resolve '@/...' -> App does not compile in ANY run mode. The plan must instruct ADDING the server.proxy key to the existing config, preserving resolve.alias, not replacing the file.

PLAN grounding is factually wrong about the test harness AND blind to an existing App-rendering test that async storage will break. §0 and §7 both assert 'No DOM test harness installed (no @testing-library/*, no jsdom, no happy-dom in node_modules)' and §7 says T6/T8 'require ADDING happy-dom + @testing-library/react'. FALSE: package.json devDeps (verified) already include happy-dom 15.11.7, @testing-library/react 16.3.2, @testing-library/dom, @testing-library/jest-dom. Worse, tests/App.a11y.test.jsx ALREADY EXISTS and calls render(<App/>) ~14 times (lines 45,54,64,78,90,...). Once storage.js becomes async fetch, every render(<App/>) fires loadTodos()->fetch('/api/todos') on mount (no server in the test env) and saveTodos()->PUT on each add. The plan's INV-A tripwire and §8 non-goals only guard 'src/todos.js' and 'the 87 tests in todos.test.js' and never mention App.a11y.test.jsx. The async load resolves AFTER the synchronous fireEvent assertions, causing setTodos-outside-act warnings/failures and unhandled work in a file the plan claims is untouched. The plan must (a) correct the harness-not-installed claim and (b) specify how the existing App.a11y.test.jsx keeps passing under the async seam (e.g. a fetch stub/setup).

PLAN §2.4 body-size cap does NOT actually prevent the OOM it claims (INV-L 'rejected before buffering' is unmet by the described mechanism). §2.4 step 1 says: reject 413 if Content-Length > 1MB 'before reading', then 'also enforce during/after read: if read body length > cap -> 413. Never buffer an unbounded body.' Two holes verified against Bun 1.3.11: (1) Content-Length is ABSENT on chunked Transfer-Encoding requests (curl -H 'Transfer-Encoding: chunked' gives req.headers.get('Content-Length')===null), so the pre-read check is bypassed entirely by an attacker. (2) The only body-read primitives the plan implies (req.json()/req.text()) BUFFER THE ENTIRE BODY into memory before returning -- I confirmed a 2MB chunked PUT is fully buffered (actualLen 2097153) before any 'after-read' length check can run. So the 'after read' check fires only after the OOM-sized body is already in memory: it rejects the response but does not prevent the buffering DoS INV-L exists to stop. To truly cap before buffering, the builder must stream req.body via getReader() and abort once the running byte count exceeds 1MB -- a mechanism the plan never specifies. As written a builder will 'follow the plan' with await req.json() and ship the exact vulnerability INV-L forbids.

PLAN §4.2 hydration guard silently DROPS and then OVERWRITES a user edit made during the pre-hydration window (lost update). The load flash renders the fully-interactive empty-list DOM (spec Q4 adds no spinner and does not disable the input), so a user can add/type a todo before loadTodos() resolves. Trace the plan's exact code: (1) mount todos=[], hydratedRef=false. (2) User submits 'buy milk' -> setTodos([milk]); the save effect runs but hydratedRef.current===false -> RETURNS, no PUT: the user's real edit is never persisted. (3) load resolves -> skipNextSaveRef=true; setTodos(sanitizeTodos(serverResult)) -> the user's 'buy milk' is CLOBBERED by the loaded list on screen; hydratedRef=true; the ensuing save effect sees skipNextSaveRef -> skips. Net: the todo the user visibly added vanishes from both the UI and the DB. On a warm localhost this window is small, but on first-run DB creation / cold server start / the 3000ms-timeout fallback path it is large and the loss is deterministic. This is a genuine behavior regression (INV-B: an add whose result disappears) that the guard introduces; the plan must address the edit-during-hydration case (e.g. if a user mutation occurred pre-hydration, do not clobber it / flush it) rather than assuming no interaction happens before load settles.

PLAN dev script masks a hard server-startup failure as a silently non-persisting app (INV-I hole). §3.2 'dev' = 'bun server/index.js & vite'. If port 8787 is already in use (CI, a leftover server, another project) Bun.serve throws EADDRINUSE and the backgrounded API process EXITS immediately; but '&' discards its failure and 'vite' still starts on 5173. The proxy then forwards /api to a dead 8787 -> every load/save gets a connection-refused, which loadTodos/saveTodos SWALLOW to []/false by design (INV-F). Result: 'bun run dev' comes up looking fine but persists NOTHING and shows no error -- exactly the INV-I 'silently no-ops persistence' failure the spec forbids, now reachable by a mere port conflict. The plan neither picks a free port, nor fails fast on bind error, nor verifies the API is listening before/after launching Vite. §9 weak-point-2 only discusses Ctrl-C orphaning, not startup failure. The plan should require the server to fail loudly on bind error and the dev workflow to detect a dead API (e.g. readiness check), so a port clash is not silently indistinguishable from a working app.

PLAN §2.4 Origin allowlist hardcodes port 5173 and will 403-block the LEGITIMATE app whenever Vite's dev port shifts. Vite's strictPort defaults to false, so if 5173 is busy Vite silently binds 5174/5175/... The plan's §3.1 proxy config does NOT set server.strictPort, so this fallback is live. The browser then loads the app from http://localhost:5174 and every same-origin PUT carries Origin: http://localhost:5174 -- which is NOT in the plan's fixed allowlist {127.0.0.1:8787, localhost:8787, 127.0.0.1:5173, localhost:5173}. Per §2.4 the guard rejects a PRESENT Origin not on the list -> 403 -> saveTodos->false -> the real user's edits stop persisting through the dev proxy, a self-inflicted denial of the legit app (violates 'must not block the legitimate same-origin app'). Same class breaks integration tests using TODOS_PORT on a temp port (§7): the server runs on port X but the allowlist has no origin for X. The plan must either pin the dev port (server.strictPort:true + a fixed proxy) or derive the allowlist from the configured port(s) rather than a hardcoded literal set.

PLAN hardcodes port 8787 in three places that can drift out of sync, and the TODOS_PORT test-affordance only reaches one of them. §7 requires the server to read TODOS_PORT (default 8787) so integration tests can use a temp port. But the Vite dev proxy target (§3.1: 'http://127.0.0.1:8787') and the preview flow are hardcoded to 8787 and do NOT read TODOS_PORT. So a dev/test run with TODOS_PORT set moves the server off 8787 while the proxy still targets 8787 -> /api 404s. And T9 (INV-I dev-reachability check) that starts the dev proxy cannot use TODOS_PORT without also patching the proxy. The plan should define a single source of truth for the port (e.g. proxy target reads the same env/const the server binds) so the '8787' literal is not duplicated across server bind + proxy target + preview with independent drift.

PLAN §4.2 uses sanitizeTodos() in App.jsx but never adds the import, and its 'only lines 53 and 62-64 change' rule actively steers the builder away from fixing it. The load effect in §4.2 calls setTodos(sanitizeTodos(result)) (line 289), but the live App.jsx import block (lines 12-21) imports {addTodo, toggleTodo, editTodo, deleteTodo, clearCompleted, filterTodos, remainingCount, makeId} from './todos.js' -- sanitizeTodos is NOT among them (verified: grep finds no sanitizeTodos in App.jsx). The plan repeatedly asserts the ONLY App.jsx changes are line 53 and the 62-64 region and 'all markup and handlers stay byte-identical', so a builder following it literally will NOT edit the import line -> ReferenceError: sanitizeTodos is not defined thrown inside the load effect on every mount -> app crashes on load in all run modes. (Separately, this sanitize is redundant since storage.js's loadTodos already returns sanitizeTodos(data) per §4.1 -- the cleaner fix is to drop the extra call, but as written the plan both needs an import it doesn't add and double-sanitizes.) The plan must either add sanitizeTodos to the App.jsx import list explicitly or remove the redundant call.

PLAN §7 T8 says 'repeat under a simulated StrictMode double-mount' to prove INV-H, but gives the verifier no mechanism, and the existing harness renders bare <App/> (no StrictMode). tests/App.a11y.test.jsx (the installed harness) calls render(<App/>) directly -- React only double-invokes effects when the tree is wrapped in <React.StrictMode>, which happens in main.jsx, NOT in a bare test render. So a verifier who 'repeats' T8 by rendering <App/> twice does NOT reproduce the mount->unmount->remount double-invoke that INV-H's abort/ignore guard exists to survive; the test would pass without ever exercising the StrictMode path. The plan should specify that T8 wraps the render in <React.StrictMode> (or otherwise forces the double-invoke) and asserts no PUT and no setState-after-unmount, so INV-H's StrictMode clause is actually proven rather than nominally checked.

PLAN §2.4 Origin/Host guard fails OPEN when BOTH headers are absent. The two rules are: (a) reject 403 if Origin is PRESENT and not allow-listed; (b) reject 403 if Host is PRESENT and its hostname is not 127.0.0.1/localhost; and 'a missing Origin is allowed only if the Host check passes.' A request with NO Origin AND NO Host header satisfies neither rejection clause: Origin-absent skips (a), Host-absent skips (b) (it only rejects a PRESENT non-loopback Host), and the Host check vacuously 'passes' -> the write is ALLOWED. Browsers always send Host, but a non-browser client (crafted socket, an SSRF/proxy pivot that strips headers, curl --http1.0 without Host) can PUT with neither header and overwrite/wipe the todo file -- the exact 'unauthenticated local write' INV-L is meant to gate. The guard should default-DENY: require Host to be present AND loopback (reject if absent), not merely reject a present-foreign Host.

VALID as filed; now adequately resolved (rejected=closed per this ledger's convention). Verified live vite.config.js:7-11 has resolve.alias '@'->./src and App.jsx:23-26 depend on it. Plan revision §3.1 gives the full MERGED config preserving resolve.alias + node:url imports verbatim and adds the proxy; §6 step 4 explicitly says 'MERGE, not a replacement' and 'confirm resolve.alias is present'. Build no longer breaks.

VALID as filed; now resolved. Confirmed live package.json:22-34 already has happy-dom 15.11.7 + @testing-library/{react,dom,jest-dom}, and tests/App.a11y.test.jsx renders <App/> repeatedly with localStorage.clear(). Plan §0 retracts the false 'no harness' claim; §4.3 gives a concrete, workable fix: install a globalThis.fetch stub in beforeEach (GET->{ok:true,json:()=>[]}, PUT->{ok:true}), vi.unstubAllGlobals() in afterEach, and await waitFor/findBy plus async addTodo helper so the async seam settles inside act. Not a vague gesture.

VALID as filed; now resolved. Plan §1 + §2.4 step 1 specify a real before-buffering mechanism: reject 411 on absent Content-Length (chunked bodies), reject 413 immediately when Content-Length>1048576 before reading, then stream the body via req.body getReader()/for-await maintaining a running byte total and abort+413 the instant it exceeds the cap; req.json()/req.text() are explicitly forbidden because they fully buffer first. This satisfies INV-L's 'rejected before buffering' as a real property, not an after-the-fact status.

VALID as filed; now resolved. Plan §4.2 introduces editedBeforeHydrationRef: the pre-hydration save effect sets it when todos.length>0 (the only way to grow from mount []=a real user add); the load resolver checks the flag and does NOT setTodos(serverResult) (no on-screen clobber), instead re-commits current.slice() so the now-hydrated save effect emits exactly one PUT carrying the user's edit. The added todo survives in both UI and DB via a concrete mechanism, not an assertion. §9 point 6 honestly names the length-heuristic residual.

VALID as filed; now resolved. Plan §2.3 makes Bun.serve propagate EADDRINUSE so the process exits non-zero and logs a readiness marker; §3.2 replaces the bare '& vite' with scripts/dev.mjs which spawns the server, polls GET /api/todos for readiness, and if the child exits or readiness times out prints the error and exits non-zero WITHOUT starting Vite; preview replaces 'vite preview' with the Bun server which also fails loud on bind error. Concrete script described, uses only built-ins. A port clash is now a loud non-zero failure, not a silently non-persisting app.

VALID as filed; now resolved. Plan §3.1 sets server.strictPort:true so the dev origin is pinned to :5173 (Vite fails fast rather than shifting to 5174), and §2.4 step 2 derives the Origin allowlist from PORT + the pinned 5173 rather than hardcoded literals. A temp TODOS_PORT run gets a matching allowlist entry automatically. The self-inflicted 403 on the legit app is closed robustly.

VALID as filed (minor); now resolved. Plan §2.3 defines PORT=Number(process.env.TODOS_PORT)||8787 as the single source of truth; §3.1 Vite proxy target reads the same TODOS_PORT; §3.2 preview flow reads the same TODOS_PORT; §7 confirms all consumers (bind, proxy, preview, readiness helper) derive from it. No duplicated 8787 literal that can drift. One clear source of truth consumed consistently.

VALID as filed; now resolved. Grep of live App.jsx confirms sanitizeTodos is NOT imported (import block lines 12-21). Plan §0 + §4.2 fix this by DROPPING the redundant sanitize call in App.jsx (storage.js loadTodos already returns sanitizeTodos(data) per §4.1), so App.jsx adds NO new import and the 'only the seam changes' framing holds; §6 step 6 reinforces 'Do NOT add any import'. This avoids the ReferenceError without a silently-required import. Correct resolution.

VALID as filed (minor); now resolved. Confirmed main.jsx:7-9 wraps <App/> in <React.StrictMode> while the test renders bare <App/>. Plan §7 T8 now explicitly requires render(<React.StrictMode><App/></React.StrictMode>) and states a bare render(<App/>) does NOT double-invoke effects and would falsely pass. The double-invoke mechanism is specified for the verifier.

VALID as filed (minor); now resolved. Plan §2.4 step 2 makes the guard default-DENY: Host MUST be present AND loopback, so a request with no Host at all is rejected (403), not vacuously allowed; Origin-absent is tolerated only because the mandatory Host check already gates it. T11 explicitly tests 'no Host and no Origin -> 403'. The both-headers-absent fail-open hole is closed.

Pre-hydration add-then-delete wipes the persisted server list (data loss; falsifies INV-H intent). Repro: returning user has a non-empty list on the server; GET is still in flight (the <=200ms/3000ms flash). User adds a todo then deletes it (net back to empty) during the flash. In App.jsx the pre-hydration save effect latches editedBeforeHydrationRef=true when todos.length>0 (App.jsx:95) and NEVER resets it when the list returns to empty. When loadTodos() resolves (App.jsx:70), the resolver sees the latched flag, SKIPS applying the server list, and re-commits current.slice()===[] which the now-hydrated save effect PUTs as []. Net: server list [X,Y] is overwritten with [] and the UI shows empty. Proven by a happy-dom component test (slow GET returning [{id:server1}], add 'flashadd' then delete during flash, resolve GET): observed PUT bodies=[[]] and 'shows server todo'=false. The plan §9 weakpoint #6 asserted this case 'has no user data to lose' — that reasoning is wrong because the server DID hold data and the latched flag diverts to the clobber branch. INV-H/§9 requires mount+no-net-edit to never overwrite the persisted list.

No-Host request hits Bun's URL parser (400) before the write guard's default-deny branch runs, making checkWriteGuard's host===null->403 path (server/index.js:139-141) dead code and failing plan T11's specified assertion. handler() computes new URL(req.url) at server/index.js:199 OUTSIDE the try/catch (try starts at :202). When a request omits Host, Bun sets req.url to the relative '/api/todos', which 'cannot be parsed as a URL', so new URL() throws and Bun returns its own 400 before method dispatch or the guard ever runs. Verified via raw socket: both 'PUT /api/todos HTTP/1.1' and 'GET /api/todos HTTP/1.1' with NO Host header return HTTP/1.1 400 (proving it's the URL parse, not the 403 guard). Impact: INV-L's write-BLOCK still holds (400 blocks the write; old list intact — confirmed unchanged), so this is not a security hole; but plan T11 explicitly requires 'PUT with NO Host and NO Origin -> 403 (default-deny, F70)', which the implementation cannot satisfy — the verifier's T11 case as written will fail, and the intended default-deny code is unreachable. Fix: move new URL(req.url) inside the try, or derive pathname without throwing, so the guard's 403 path is actually reachable.

README.md is stale and now factually wrong after the SQLite/server change (plan §3.2 called for updating the 'Run it' block; it was not touched). README.md:3-5 still says 'client-only', 'State persists to localStorage', 'No backend, accounts, or network' — all false now that persistence requires the Bun server process. README.md:14 says '51 test() blocks' (suite is now 101 tests across 2 files). README.md:26 calls src/storage.js a 'localStorage adapter'; it is now an async fetch adapter to /api/todos. No spec invariant is violated (INV-B is UI-only, docs are out of its scope), but the docs actively mislead a reader into thinking no server is needed. Low impact; flagged for accuracy.

Root cause: editedBeforeHydrationRef was a STICKY latch (App.jsx:95 set it true on the first pre-hydration non-empty list and never reset it), so the load resolver's preserve-branch fired even when the in-memory list had netted back to empty (add-then-delete during the flash), re-committing current.slice()===[] as a destructive PUT [] that wiped a real non-empty server list. Fix (App.jsx): replace the sticky latch with preHydrationNonEmptyRef that TRACKS the current in-memory list's non-emptiness on every pre-hydration save-effect run (set to todos.length>0, resetting to false when the list returns to empty). The resolver now takes the preserve/flush branch ONLY when the current in-memory list is genuinely non-empty at resolution time; an add-then-delete resets the flag and falls through to the normal skipNextSaveRef path that APPLIES the loaded server data and emits no PUT. The original guard is intact: mount [] keeps the flag false -> applies server data, never an early PUT []; a live non-empty pre-hydration edit (F64) still preserves the user's list and flushes exactly one PUT carrying it.

Added tests/App.hydration-f33.test.jsx: renders <StrictMode><App/></StrictMode> with a controlled/deferred GET stub (blocks until the test resolves the flash) and a PUT-recording spy. Case 1 (F33 repro): pre-seed server list [{id:server1,'real server todo'}], during the flash add 'flashadd' then delete it (net empty), then resolve the GET -> asserts 'real server todo' is shown (server list survived) AND zero PUT [] bodies were emitted. Case 2 (F64 not regressed): add 'keepme' during the flash, keep it, resolve GET -> asserts 'keepme' preserved, server list NOT applied over it, exactly one non-empty PUT carrying 'keepme', zero empty PUTs. Proved the test is a genuine repro: reverting App.jsx to the sticky-latch logic makes Case 1 FAIL (server list wiped, 'real server todo' never appears) while Case 2 still passes; with the fix both pass. Full suite green: 103 tests (bun run test), including the pre-existing 87 core + 14 a11y.

INV-L (block unauthenticated/cross-origin writes) HOLDS — the finding itself concedes 'this is not a security hole; 400 blocks the write, old list intact.' The control point for the no-Host case is simply stricter than the app guard: Bun's HTTP/1.1 server enforces RFC 7230's mandatory Host header at the protocol layer and returns 400 WITHOUT EVER INVOKING our fetch handler. Verified directly: a Bun.serve whose fetch() logs on entry is never called for a raw 'GET/PUT /api/todos HTTP/1.1' with no Host header (no HANDLER-INVOKED log; client gets HTTP/1.1 400). I also tried moving/basing the URL parse (new URL(req.url,'http://127.0.0.1')) to reach dispatch — it cannot help, because the handler is never entered. So the app-layer checkWriteGuard host===null->403 branch (server/index.js:139-141) is unreachable-by-construction defense-in-depth: harmless to keep as documented intent, and correct to leave since there is no way to make Bun deliver a Host-less request to app code. The only real defect is the PLAN's T11 wording ('PUT with NO Host and NO Origin -> 403'), which targets the wrong layer; the satisfiable, equivalent assertion is 'a Host-less write is BLOCKED (4xx) and the DB is unmutated', which the implementation already guarantees via the 400. Severity minor; no code change is warranted because the invariant is already enforced by a stricter control than the one the finding asks us to make reachable.

Updated README.md to match the shipped ISSUE-15 architecture. Intro (lines 3-5): dropped the false 'client-only' / 'persists to localStorage' / 'No backend, accounts, or network' and replaced with 'persists across reloads via a small local Bun API backed by SQLite (loopback-only, no accounts or multi-user)'. Run-it block: removed the stale hardcoded '(51 test() blocks)' count (now just 'Vitest suite') so it can't drift again. Architecture table: src/storage.js description changed from 'Thin localStorage adapter' to 'Async persistence adapter — fetches the whole list to/from the local /api/todos endpoint; never rejects (maps every failure to a safe fallback)', and added a server/index.js row (loopback-only Bun HTTP server owning the SQLite file, serving GET/PUT /api/todos and dist/ in preview/prod). Kept it brief and factual; no invariant was at stake (INV-B is UI-only) but the docs no longer mislead a reader into thinking no server is needed.

Finding VALID (data-loss, correctly filed blocker) and the fix is adequate + proven. Independently traced against src/App.jsx:61,70-88,97-105. The sticky editedBeforeHydrationRef latch is replaced by preHydrationNonEmptyRef, recomputed on EVERY pre-hydration todos change (line 104: preHydrationNonEmptyRef.current = todos.length>0). Repro trace: mount[] -> add(ref=true) -> delete(ref=false); GET resolves reading ref===false -> skips the F64 keep-in-memory branch, sets skipNextSaveRef and applies the server list. No PUT [] is ever emitted and [server1] survives. Non-regression confirmed both ways: (a) F64 add-that-stays-nonempty still routes to the keep-in-memory branch and flushes exactly one PUT of the user's edit (ref===true); (b) the ORIGINAL mount-wipe bug (F50/F51: healthy-but-slow mount, no edit) is NOT reintroduced — pre-hydration [] leaves ref=false and hydratedRef still suppresses all pre-hydration PUTs, so the resolver applies the server list with no early destructive PUT []. tests/App.hydration-f33.test.jsx exercises both the repro (net-empty during flash) and the F64 case under StrictMode; full suite green (103 passed). Fixed+tested already satisfies the gate; this verdict is the confirmatory record.

REBUTTAL STANDS (finding filed minor; correctly not gate-holding). Independently reproduced: started server/index.js on a temp port/DB and sent raw HTTP/1.1 with NO Host header via nc — both 'PUT /api/todos' and 'GET /api/todos' return HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request (Bun's own URL parser, since new URL(req.url) at server/index.js:199 sits OUTSIDE the try and throws on the relative req.url Bun produces when Host is absent). A control PUT WITH Host and no Origin returns 200, confirming it is the URL parse, not the guard. Crucially INV-L's actual security property — the destructive write is BLOCKED and the old list is intact — holds regardless (400 blocks the write; verified data unchanged). The defender is right that the security invariant is satisfied and that plan T11's specific '403 default-deny' status-code expectation was over-specified against a real HTTP-layer constraint the plan did not anticipate. So the finding does not hold the gate. NON-BLOCKING FOLLOW-UP (not required for merge): the checkWriteGuard host===null->403 branch (server/index.js:139-141) is genuinely unreachable dead code, and T11's wording should be reconciled — either move new URL inside the try / derive pathname without throwing so the 403 path is exercised, OR amend T11 to assert 'write blocked (400 or 403)'. Recommend the architect/verifier note this for a later pass; it is a plan/test-guidance nit, not a defect that blocks this item.

Finding VALID (correctly filed nit; never gate-holding) and fix adequate. Verified README.md: the false claims are gone — line 5 now states persistence is 'via a small local Bun API backed by SQLite (loopback-only)' (no more 'client-only'/'localStorage'/'no backend'); line 27 describes src/storage.js as an 'Async persistence adapter' that fetches to/from '/api/todos' (no longer 'localStorage adapter'); line 30 documents server/index.js as the loopback-only Bun HTTP server owning the SQLite file. The stale hardcoded test-count claim is also removed. Docs now reasonably describe the current client+server architecture.

R6/T4 conflates shape-rejection with constraint-rollback. Spec frames empty-id AND empty-text AND duplicate-id all as 'constraint-violation rollback (INV-K)' cases exercising the transactional DELETE-then-rollback path. Verified against server/index.js: empty-id (isConformingList line 95: el.id.length===0) and empty-text (line 96) are rejected by SHAPE validation at line 245 -> 400 BEFORE replaceAll is ever called (line 251), so the DB is never touched and the transaction/rollback path is never entered. Only duplicate-id actually reaches replaceAll and triggers the SQLite PRIMARY KEY rollback (lines 251-256). A verifier writing T4 per R6 will believe empty-id/empty-text prove rollback when they only prove shape-rejection; the 'old list intact' assertion passes trivially (DB untouched) and gives false confidence. R6 must split shape-rejection (empty id/text) from true rollback (dup-id, and ideally a completed-not-boolean-at-DB or other CHECK path) so the rollback path has a test that actually enters it.

R10(c) falsify criterion under-specifies return values under coalescing. Spec: 'two rapid saveTodos(v1) then saveTodos(v2) leave the server holding v2'. Verified empirically against real server: server DOES hold v2 (correct), BUT saveTodos(v2) RETURNS false, not true, because storage.js line 56 returns false immediately when a drain loop is already running (sending===true); the first call's loop picks up the coalesced pending=v2. Result: saveTodos(v1)===true, saveTodos(v2)===false, server=v2. R10(c)/T8(c) as written is satisfiable, but a verifier who additionally asserts the second save's boolean return is true will get a FALSE failure. Spec should state explicitly that under coalescing only the drain-owning call reports the final result and the coalesced caller may return false; the binding assertion is the GET, not the second return value.

D2/R11/Q3 'OS-assigned free port' has an unaddressed TOCTOU race that makes the suite flaky, not collision-free as claimed. The stated mechanism (Q3 line 419: 'grab an ephemeral port, close it, pass the number') opens a window between closing the probe socket and the spawned server binding it; another process (or a parallel Vitest worker running a second integration file) can grab that port in the gap. server/index.js line 282-286 binds hard and Bun.serve THROWS synchronously on EADDRINUSE (documented at line 280-281), so a lost race crashes the child -> the test fails loudly but SPURIOUSLY. The spec presents OS-assigned port as strictly collision-avoiding (INV-C 'ideally OS-assigned') without noting the race or requiring a bind-retry/backoff. Vitest also runs test files in parallel by default; two integration files each doing probe-then-spawn compound the risk. Plan should mandate either a retry-on-EADDRINUSE spawn loop or file-level serialization of integration suites (e.g. a single shared server fixture / poolOptions singleFork), and R11's falsify list should include the parallel-worker port-race case.

Split R6 (and T4) into R6a/R6b to stop conflating shape-rejection with constraint-rollback. R6a covers empty-id/empty-text: isConformingList (server/index.js:95-96) rejects them at validation (line 245) with 400 BEFORE replaceAll runs, so the DB is untouched by construction, not by rollback; R6a's falsify list and a verifier note make explicit it does NOT and cannot prove rollback (no DELETE occurs). R6b covers duplicate-id: it is shape-valid, passes isConformingList, reaches replaceAll (line 251), runs DELETE FROM todos, trips the id PRIMARY KEY constraint, and auto-rolls-back (lines 250-256) leaving the OLD list intact -- the only true INV-K rollback case. Test map now has T4a->R6a (validation) and T4b->R6b (DELETE-then-rollback), each with distinct falsify criteria.

R10(c)/T8(c): made explicit that under save-coalescing (src/storage.js:54-57), when saveTodos(v1) then saveTodos(v2) race, the coalesced second call returns false immediately (line 56) while the drain loop owned by the first call still PUTs v2 and the server correctly ends holding v2. Added a binding rule: the INV-J assertion is the server's FINAL GET state (== v2), NOT the coalesced call's boolean return; a false return from the coalesced call is expected/correct behavior, and a test that fails on it is itself a defect. R10(a)'s single non-coalesced save may still assert its true return. T8(c) updated to assert the real GET === v2, not the boolean.

Closed the port-acquisition TOCTOU race. §0 now records the verified fact that with no vitest.config.* and no test block in vite.config.js, Vitest runs test files in PARALLEL by default, so parallel workers can race for a port; Bun.serve throws synchronously on EADDRINUSE (server/index.js:282-286), crashing the child spuriously. D2 now MANDATES one of: (A) a retry-on-EADDRINUSE spawn loop that re-picks a fresh port on bind failure up to a bounded attempt count (preferred, no config change), or (B) serializing the integration files via a permitted vitest.config.*/test block (poolOptions singleFork) / a single shared server fixture. Added R11(f) and the parallel-worker port-race case to R11's falsify list; INV-C now requires race-safe acquisition; N1/INV-D/Q6 permit the serialization-only config file; Q3 recast around the race; T9 must exercise race-safety.

Valid (filed major); now resolved. Revised spec splits R6 into R6a (shape-rejection: empty-id line 95 / empty-text line 96 fail isConformingList at server/index.js:245, rejected 400 BEFORE replaceAll at :251 — DB untouched by construction, not rollback; R6a explicitly notes it cannot distinguish validation from rollback and forbids framing it as a rollback proof) and R6b (duplicate-id is shape-valid, passes isConformingList, reaches replaceAll, trips PRIMARY KEY, auto-rolls-back per :250-256 — the only case exercising DELETE-then-rollback/INV-K). T4a/T4b mirror the split. The false-confidence hole is closed.

Valid (filed minor); now resolved. R10(c) constraint and T8(c) now state that under save-coalescing the second saveTodos returns false immediately (src/storage.js:56 'if (sending) return false'), that a false return from the coalesced call is expected/correct behavior not a failure, and that the binding INV-J assertion is the server's final GET state (== v2), NOT the second call's boolean. This forecloses the spurious verifier failure.

Valid (filed minor); now resolved. D2, R11(f), INV-C and Q3 now require race-safe port acquisition (retry-on-EADDRINUSE spawn loop (A) or serialized integration files (B)), grounded in the verified fact that Bun.serve throws synchronously on EADDRINUSE (server/index.js:280-286) and Vitest runs test files in parallel by default (§0). The naive grab-close-pass TOCTOU is called out and the falsify list includes the parallel-worker port-race case.

Independently verified the R6a/R6b split in docs/specs/ISSUE-19.md against the REAL server/index.js (not just spec prose), by static reading + spawning bun server/index.js on a throwaway TODOS_PORT/TODOS_DB_PATH and driving it over real HTTP. Control flow confirmed: isConformingList (lines 91-100) runs at line 245 BEFORE replaceAll at line 251. R6a: empty-id (line 95) and empty-text (line 96) each fail isConformingList and return 400 'bad request' at line 246 with replaceAll/DELETE never reached -- follow-up GET returned the original list unchanged, proving 'DB untouched by construction' (not by rollback). R6b: duplicate-id is shape-valid, passes isConformingList, reaches replaceAll (DELETE FROM todos executes), then trips id TEXT PRIMARY KEY on insert; the transaction auto-rolls-back and the catch at 252-256 returns 400 'constraint violation' -- follow-up GET returned the exact original list, proving the DELETE-then-rollback path (lines 250-256) restored state. Decisive path-distinguishing evidence: the two cases return DIFFERENT error strings ('bad request' from the validation short-circuit vs 'constraint violation' from the post-DELETE rollback catch), confirming duplicate-id is the ONLY body that reaches replaceAll and exercises rollback -- exactly as the split isolates. Server stayed responsive (200) after all three; no data/todos.sqlite created/touched (INV-B), no leaked process, throwaway DB removed, zero repo footprint (git tree clean, no test files landed -- spec phase).

R9(c) non-loopback-Host test is UNPROVABLE with the plan's mandated tooling (platform fetch), and the plan's fallback does not actually cover the requirement. Verified empirically: Node/undici fetch SILENTLY IGNORES a user-supplied Host header — a PUT with headers:{Host:'evil.example'} sends the real Host:127.0.0.1:<port>, never the forged one (probe: request arrives with host='127.0.0.1:<port>', not 'evil.example'). So plan §6 T7(c) ('putTodos([...],{headers:{Host:'evil.example'}}) -> 403') can never trigger checkWriteGuard's non-loopback-Host branch (server line 143); it will hit the success/same-origin path instead and the assertion of 403 will FAIL, or worse silently mutate the DB if the builder softens the assertion. Spec R9 explicitly requires 'A PUT with a non-loopback Host header is likewise 403.' The plan's §6-T7 / §9-#2 hedge ('if truly un-settable, rely on the Origin case + the missing-both 403 covered at server level') does NOT cover it: fetch also ALWAYS sets Host, so a missing-Host request is equally unproducible via fetch — there is no fetch path that exercises the Host branch at all. Because INV-G/N6 forbids new deps and the plan's request helpers (§4.6 rawPut) are all fetch-based, R9's Host branch has no implementable test as planned. The plan must either (a) permit a raw node:net socket write for the Host/missing-Host guard cases (built-in, not a heavyweight dep) and specify it, or (b) explicitly rescope R9(c) — but it cannot claim coverage it cannot produce.

R8/T6(a) 'declared over-cap Content-Length -> 413 before body read' is UNIMPLEMENTABLE with platform fetch, and T6(b) 'streamed cap' is an UNREACHABLE distinct path — the plan's two R8 mechanisms are wrong and will make two builders diverge or produce a non-falsifiable test. Verified empirically: (1) A fetch with a hand-set headers:{'Content-Length':'99999999'} and a small body THROWS 'TypeError: fetch failed' / cause UND_ERR_REQ_CONTENT_LENGTH_MISMATCH ('Request body length does not match content-length header') — undici refuses to send a Content-Length that disagrees with the actual body. So plan §6-T6(a) ('send a PUT with a Content-Length header declaring > 1_048_576') never reaches the server; it errors client-side and cannot assert the server's 413 (server line 222). (2) Because fetch always sets an ACCURATE Content-Length from the body, any body that actually exceeds 1 MB is rejected at server line 222 (declared > MAX_BODY_BYTES -> 413) BEFORE the streaming reader runs — so the mid-stream cap at server line 234 (readCappedBody returning null) is UNREACHABLE through fetch. T6(b) as written ('a body whose actual JSON string exceeds 1 MB -> 413 (streamed cap)') does hit 413, but via the declared-length branch, NOT the streamed branch it claims to exercise; the two sub-cases (a) and (b) collapse into the same code path and the 'streamed cap' path stays untested. R8's requirement (over-cap -> 413, not persisted) IS provable via a single real >1 MB body, but the plan's specified breakdown is factually wrong; it must (a) drop the fake-Content-Length T6(a) mechanism, and (b) either use a raw socket to exercise the true streamed mid-stream cap or explicitly acknowledge that branch is unreachable via fetch and rescope. As written the builder implementing T6(a) verbatim gets a test that throws before it asserts anything.

Grounding error: Vitest 1.6.1's default pool is 'threads' (worker_threads), NOT 'forks'. The plan asserts 'forks' repeatedly — §0 ('default forks pool, files in parallel'), §1 Q3 table, and §0-tests ('default forks pool'). Verified: node_modules/vitest is 1.6.1 and its config default is pool:'threads' (vitest/dist/config.cjs: pool: "threads"). In Vitest 1.x the default pool is threads; forks only became default in Vitest 2.x. Impact: the chosen fix (option A, retry-on-EADDRINUSE) is unaffected, so this is not a blocker. BUT the plan's serialization ALTERNATIVE (D2(B), cited in §4.3 and §1) names 'poolOptions.forks.singleFork' as the serialization knob — under the actual default 'threads' pool that option is a NO-OP; the correct knobs would be poolOptions.threads.singleThread or fileParallelism:false. If the arbiter or a future builder ever falls back to option B (or the retry loop proves insufficient), the plan's stated config would silently fail to serialize. The grounding should be corrected to 'threads' and the option-B knob names fixed, so the fallback isn't a trap. Also note: parallel file execution still happens under threads (still real for R11(f)), so the port-race premise stands — only the pool NAME and the singleFork knob are wrong.

The §4.5 crash-safety net cannot remove the temp DB on abnormal exit, so INV-B ('no stray temp DB after teardown') can be violated on a crash — the one path the net exists to cover. Plan §4.5: 'register a process.once("exit", ...) handler that best-effort-kills any still-live child, so a thrown assertion cannot leak a listening Bun process or a locked temp DB even if afterAll is skipped.' Node's 'exit' event handler runs SYNCHRONOUSLY and the event loop is already stopped — only synchronous work executes; any async I/O (fs.promises.rm, or even a queued microtask) is silently dropped (verified: an async body in a process.once('exit') callback does not complete). child.kill('SIGTERM') is synchronous so the process-leak half works, BUT the plan's chosen teardown deletes the temp DIR via async rm(dbDir,{recursive:true,force:true}) (§4.2) — that cannot run in an exit handler. On a hard crash / uncaught exception path where afterAll is skipped, the child is killed but the <os-tmpdir>/todos-int-<random> directory (db.sqlite + -wal + -shm) is LEFT BEHIND, violating INV-B/R11 ('no stray temp DB'). The plan must specify a SYNCHRONOUS cleanup (fs.rmSync) in the exit handler, or accept that the crash path leaks a temp dir and adjust INV-B's teardown guarantee. As written, two builders diverge: one uses async rm in the exit net (leaks), one uses rmSync (correct); the plan doesn't pin it.

The §4.3/§4.4 EADDRINUSE-retry fixture has a readiness-vs-foreign-server race that can bind a test to the WRONG server, defeating the very F3 fix that is the plan's centerpiece. Sequence: (1) probe opens port 0, gets port P, closes it (§4.3 step 1); (2) a PARALLEL Vitest worker's integration server binds P in the TOCTOU window and is HEALTHY on P; (3) our child spawns with TODOS_PORT=P, tries to bind P, gets EADDRINUSE, and exits (§0/server line 282). The plan detects (3) via the child 'exit' event and retries — GOOD. BUT §4.4 runs the readiness poll ('GET http://127.0.0.1:P/api/todos until res.ok') CONCURRENTLY with the exit watch. That GET does not know which process answers port P: it will get a 200 from the OTHER worker's healthy server on P. If that 200 arrives before our dead child's 'exit' event is delivered (both are async; ordering is not guaranteed — a loopback 200 can resolve in <1ms while process 'exit' delivery is a separate tick), the fixture RESOLVES READINESS and breaks the retry loop (§4.3 step 3 'Ready -> success, keep this child') while our child is dead or dying. Result: the fixture's baseUrl points at a FOREIGN server owning a DIFFERENT temp DB; every subsequent test PUTs/GETs against the other worker's DB — cross-test contamination, nondeterministic failures, and a violation of INV-B isolation, all masquerading as passes/flakes. The plan's interlock ('if child exits while polling, abandon polling') assumes exit fires FIRST or concurrently-detectably; it does not guard the case where a healthy foreign 200 wins the race. Mitigation the plan must specify: gate readiness on OUR child's stdout marker (server line 289, which only our child prints) rather than a portable GET, AND/OR verify the child is still alive at the moment readiness resolves before accepting it. As written, the readiness signal (portable GET) cannot distinguish our server from a squatter on the same port — precisely the condition the retry loop exists to handle.

R9(c)/missing-Host reworked: fetch/undici silently forces Host so it is unprovable via fetch. Added a raw node:net socket helper rawRequest() in tests/integration/helpers/server.js (sec 4.7) that hand-writes an HTTP/1.1 request with a forged (Host: evil.example) or absent (omitHost) Host; drives T7(c) -> 403 at server lines 142/139. Built-in module, zero deps (INV-G).

Dropped the fake-Content-Length mechanism from T6(a) (undici rejects length/body mismatch client-side, never reaches server). R8 now proven with ONE real >1MB JSON body via fetch, hitting the declared-length branch server/index.js:222 -> 413 + not-persisted. True mid-stream cap (server/index.js:234, readCappedBody) is either exercised via rawRequest with a lying/omitted Content-Length (T6(d), OPTIONAL) or explicitly declared out of scope with a code comment citing F5. Rewrote T6 and weakest-point #3.

Corrected the default Vitest pool from 'forks' to 'threads' (verified against installed vitest 1.6.1: pool defaults to threads/worker_threads). Fixed every reference (sec 0, sec 4.3, T9(f), Sources) and changed the option-B serialization knob from poolOptions.forks.singleFork to poolOptions.threads.singleThread (or fileParallelism:false).

process.once('exit') crash net now uses synchronous fs.rmSync(dbDir,{recursive:true,force:true}) instead of the async rm() (exit handlers are sync-only and cannot await a promise, which leaked the temp DB dir on a hard crash). sec 4.2/4.5 updated; async rm() retained only for the normal stop() path.

EADDRINUSE-retry readiness reworked (sec 4.4): readiness is now gated FIRST on OUR child's own stdout marker (server/index.js:289 'todos API listening on http://127.0.0.1:'), matched against the child's own captured stdout buffer that no foreign process can write into, so a parallel worker squatting the same port can't be mistaken for ours. GET .../api/todos demoted to an OPTIONAL secondary confirmation run only AFTER the marker match. Pre-marker child exit is the retry trigger.

Fix resolves it. Plan §4.7 adds rawRequest() built on node:net (built-in, INV-G) that writes HTTP/1.1 bytes by hand; T7(c) (§6) drives forged Host:evil.example -> server line 142 (403) and omitHost -> server line 139 (403), the two branches fetch/undici cannot express. Grounding verified against server/index.js lines 139/142. Major as filed.

Fix resolves it. T6(a) now proves R8 with one real >1MB JSON body via fetch, hitting the declared-length branch server:222 -> 413; the fake mismatched-Content-Length mechanism is explicitly removed (undici rejects it client-side). The true mid-stream cap (server:234) is either exercised via rawRequest with lying/omitted Content-Length (T6(d), optional) or explicitly declared out of scope with an F5 comment. Coverage claim now matches the reachable code path. Major as filed.

Fix resolves it. 'forks' corrected to 'threads' throughout (§0, §1 Q3, §0-tests) matching installed vitest@1.6.1 default pool; option-B fallback knob corrected to poolOptions.threads.singleThread / fileParallelism:false (§4.3, Sources). Parallel-file premise for R11(f) correctly retained. Minor as filed.

Fix resolves it. §4.5/§4.2 crash-safety net now mandates synchronous fs.rmSync(dbDir,{recursive:true,force:true}) in the process.once('exit') handler, replacing async rm() which cannot complete in a sync exit handler. Closes the INV-B temp-dir-leak on the crash path. Minor as filed.

Fix resolves it. §4.4 makes readiness a two-stage gate where the PRIMARY signal is our child's own stdout marker (server:289, written only to our spawned process's own pipe); the GET is demoted to an optional secondary confirmation AFTER the marker match. A foreign parallel-worker server squatting the same port cannot forge our child's captured stdout, so it can no longer be mistaken for ours. Interlock races marker-seen vs child exit. Major as filed.

Proved empirically against a real spawned server/index.js (throwaway node:net + child_process harness, temp DB under os.tmpdir). Core F4 remedy PROVEN: a hand-written raw HTTP/1.1 PUT with a forged non-loopback 'Host: evil.example' (and neighbors evil.example:80, 127.0.0.1.evil.example, localhost.evil) reaches the server verbatim and checkWriteGuard returns 403 (server line 143), deterministically across 4 runs; DB unmutated (follow-up GET still shows seed); a raw request WITH a loopback Host is accepted (200) proving the 403 is the guard, not a broken client. Root cause confirmed: fetch cannot reproduce this (undici overrides Host -> the same request returns 200 with the real loopback Host), so the raw node:net socket is genuinely required (INV-G, built-in, no dep). CAVEAT for the builder: the plan's secondary claim that rawRequest({omitHost:true}) reaches the server's host===null branch (line 139) -> 403 is empirically FALSE - Bun's HTTP/1.1 parser rejects a Host-less request at the protocol layer with 400 before the handler runs (HTTP/1.0 Host-less dies at new URL(req.url) -> 500), so server line 139 is unreachable via a real request. The security OUTCOME still holds (omitted-Host write is blocked >=400 and never persists), so the accepted finding's remedy (forged-Host 403 via raw socket) is proven; only the absent-Host->403-at-line-139 detail in T7(c) should be re-scoped to assert 'write blocked (400)', not '403 at line 139'.

Proved empirically against a real spawned server/index.js. Built one conforming todo whose text is 'x'.repeat(1_100_000); JSON.stringify yields a 1,100,042-byte body (confirmed > MAX_BODY_BYTES=1,048,576). PUT via the platform fetch: undici computes an accurate Content-Length from the real body and sends it, so the server rejects at the DECLARED-LENGTH branch (server/index.js line 222) -> 413, deterministically across 4 runs. Not-persisted check PROVEN: a follow-up GET shows the DB still holds only the prior seed list, i.e. the over-cap body was never written (the guard runs before the transactional replace). Neighbor/control PROVEN: an otherwise-identical under-cap body (500,000-char text, < 1MB) is accepted -> 200, so the 413 is the cap talking, not a blanket large-body rejection. This is the single real >1MB body T6(a) specifies; it satisfies R8's observable requirement (over-cap -> 413, not persisted). The mid-stream branch (line 234) is out-of-scope-via-fetch per the plan (F5) and not required here.

Proved the stdout-marker readiness gate is sound against a foreign-squatter port race (throwaway harness, two real spawned servers). (1) Marker is real & observable: the child prints 'todos API listening on http://127.0.0.1:<port>' to ITS OWN stdout on startup, carrying the exact fixed prefix and the actually-bound port; captured off child.stdout across 4 runs. (2) Marker precedes HTTP: in the readiness-gated run, a tight GET-probe loop recorded NO successful HTTP response strictly before the marker was observed (httpWorkedBeforeMarker=false), so the marker is a valid readiness signal, not lagging behind request-serving. (3) DECISIVE F8 race proof: spawned server A (foreign squatter) binding port P, then our child B told to use the SAME port P. A portable GET to P returns 200 (from A) - which a GET-based gate would wrongly accept as 'ready', the exact F8 defect. But B hits EADDRINUSE, exits code=1, and B's OWN stdout NEVER carries the marker (bMarker=false, stderr shows EADDRINUSE). Because child.stdout is a pipe only the parent-child pair holds, no foreign process can forge the marker into B's stream. Conclusion: gating on our-child's-own-stdout marker (server line 289) reliably distinguishes 'my child is ready' from 'a stranger answered a GET on this port', and B's pre-marker exit is the unambiguous retry trigger - the plan sec.4.4 mechanism is sound.

Verifier empirically found (during F4 proof) that server/index.js line 139's 'host === null' branch is unreachable via any real request: Bun's HTTP/1.1 parser rejects a Host-less request at the protocol layer with 400 before checkWriteGuard runs, so plan T7(c)'s claim of a 403 at line 139 for the absent-Host case is factually wrong. The security outcome (hostile write blocked) still holds; only the specific status-code/line claim was incorrect.

Corrected plan sec 4.7 and T7(c): absent-Host case now asserts 'status >= 400, write blocked' instead of '403 at server line 139', with an explanatory note that Bun's HTTP parser rejects the request before the JS-level host===null check ever runs.

F1: Cloud/Worker+D1 path ships with ZERO blocking automated coverage. The only required app check is tests.yml running 'bun run test' (=vitest run), which spawns the Bun adapter (tests/integration ISSUE-19) and never touches worker/index.js or D1. The Worker/D1 contract test (test:cloud, D-TESTS/R-CLOUDTEST) is proposed NON-BLOCKING (Q-CLOUD-TEST, spec:581-584) and 'outside the default vitest glob'. But there is NO vitest config in the repo, so the default glob **/*.test.* would actually PULL tests/cloud/** into the required 'bun run test' job (which has no wrangler installed/no workerd) => that job fails/hangs; or if renamed to dodge the glob, the cloud path never runs in any blocking check. Either way acceptance criterion 6 (spec:467-468) is satisfiable with a green merge while the entire migration deliverable is untested. Must resolve Q-CLOUD-TEST to REQUIRED (and add a scoping vitest config) before build.

F2: D1 constraint classifier (R-GUARD-3 / D-STORE drift trap, spec:198-201,277) is only ever tested against wrangler-dev LOCAL D1, never real remote D1. The trap's whole premise is 'D1 throws a differently-worded error' than bun:sqlite's 'constraint' string (server/index.js:255). But wrangler dev local D1 runs on a DIFFERENT SQLite engine (miniflare) than production remote D1, so their constraint-error wording can differ. A classifier that matches local D1's message can still map a duplicate-id PUT to 500 (not 400) in PRODUCTION, and the ONLY test (test:cloud, itself non-blocking per F1) runs local. R-GUARD-3's production behavior is structurally unfalsifiable in CI as specified. Spec must require pinning the exact remote-D1 error surface (or asserting the classifier against the real driver), not local emulation.

F3: Per-PR D1 binding injection is under-specified => latent cross-environment DATA BLEED (violates INV-ISO/R-ISO, spec:422-424,546). wrangler.jsonc (§7 step 5, spec:493-495) commits d1_databases[].database_id, and 'wrangler deploy' binds by that id. Preview D1s are freshly CREATED per PR with NEW uuids (D-DEPLOY, spec:312-315). The spec only says deploy '--name todo-app-pr-N with the per-PR D1 binding' but never specifies HOW the new database_id replaces the committed one. A builder who runs 'wrangler deploy --name todo-app-pr-N' against the committed wrangler.jsonc will bind the PRODUCTION database_id => the preview Worker reads/writes PRODUCTION's list. This is the exact isolation failure R-ISO forbids, and no test in scope (ISSUE-19 never runs D1; test:cloud runs one local DB) would catch it. Spec must nail the id-injection mechanism (per-env config / generated wrangler config / --var + programmatic binding).

F4: The exact deploy commands the spec prescribes will migrate the WRONG database. D-DEPLOY (spec:308-315) and §7 steps 7-8 list 'wrangler d1 migrations apply' with NO --remote flag. Verified against Cloudflare docs: in Wrangler v4 every d1 command defaults to --local (miniflare SQLite under .wrangler/state), and --remote is REQUIRED to hit the real account DB. So CI would apply migrations to a throwaway local file on the ephemeral runner and then 'wrangler deploy' a Worker whose remote D1 has NO todos table. Every GET/PUT then 500s; src/storage.js maps 500/non-JSON to [] (storage.js:27,34) so the deployed app silently 'forgets everything' while the deploy job stays green. Spec must mandate '--remote' on every provisioning migration.

F5: Static-Assets-vs-Worker routing precedence and SPA fallback are unspecified => /api/todos can return index.html and the storage seam silently masks it. D-COMPUTE/§7 step 5 (spec:150,493-495) list only assets.directory; they never specify not_found_handling (SPA fallback) or worker-vs-assets precedence. Two concrete failures: (a) R1 needs client routes to fall back to index.html, requiring not_found_handling='single-page-application' — but that mode returns index.html 200 for ANY unmatched path, so a routing/precedence misconfig makes GET /api/todos return HTML 200; loadTodos then hits res.json() -> throws -> catch -> [] (storage.js:29-33), an app that looks 'up' but has a dead API. (b) With Static Assets, assets are served BEFORE the fetch handler unless run_worker_first/serve-directly is configured for /api; the spec never states this. Must specify the assets fallback + /api worker-first routing config.

F6: The D-SEAM claim that the two runtimes are 'provably identical on the contract by construction' (spec:226-231) and INV-CONTRACT 'the two runtimes never diverge' (spec:536-538) is falsified at D1's operational limits. A whole-list replace becomes env.DB.batch([DELETE, ...N INSERTs]) (D-STORE, spec:275). A shape-valid list up to the 1MB cap holds tens of thousands of todos (~26k at ~40 bytes each); D1 batch has statement/transaction-size limits that bun:sqlite's in-process db.transaction does not, so a large-but-valid list Bun accepts can make D1 reject the batch (5xx) => the runtimes diverge on a plausible input. No requirement bounds list size and ISSUE-19 never runs D1, so nothing catches it. Either bound the list size in isConformingList or drop the absolute 'identical by construction' claim.

F7: The same-origin guard re-derivation (D-GUARDS-2, spec:255-273) risks self-blocking the whole app if the builder reuses today's origin construction. The current guard builds allowed origins as 'http://'+host (server/index.js:27-32). On workers.dev the browser sends Origin 'https://<host>' while the reconstructed compare value would be http-schemed, so Origin!=derived-origin => 403 on the app's OWN write — exactly the self-block the spec says it avoids. The spec's comparison is ambiguously worded ('Origin's scheme+host+port == the request URL's / Host's', spec:260): the Host HEADER carries no scheme. Spec must mandate deriving the compare origin from new URL(request.url).origin (https on workers.dev), not from the Host header with an assumed scheme.

F8: Preview teardown has no orphan reconciliation; a failed/cancelled 'pull_request closed' run permanently leaks a scarce D1 slot. Teardown (D-DEPLOY, spec:316-318) is the SOLE reclaim mechanism the Q-D1-CAP mitigation depends on (spec:383-387,567-570). If that single workflow run is cancelled mid-teardown (deletes Worker but not D1), errors, or never fires cleanly, the D1 is orphaned with no GC/sweep. With the ~10-DB free cap this wedges ALL new preview provisioning, and recovery requires a manual 'wrangler d1 delete'/console step — violating R-IAC 'no console clicks' (spec:431-434) and INV-CAP's 'fail loud not silent, never a half-provisioned preview' (spec:553-555). The mitigation needs a reconciliation/GC pass (e.g. scheduled sweep of todo-preview-pr-* whose PR is closed), not just best-effort on-close deletion.

F9: R-GUARD-1's 411 (absent Content-Length) case is unfalsifiable via the proposed cloud fixture and the 'carries over unchanged' claim (D-GUARDS-1, spec:249-253; R-GUARD-1, spec:412-414) ignores edge normalization. ISSUE-19 could only reach the 411 branch via a RAW node:net socket because undici always forces a Content-Length (helpers/server.js:340-355; guard.test.js:41-52). The spec describes the cloud fixture as merely 'spawning wrangler dev' (spec:510-512) with no raw-socket helper, so it cannot send a length-absent PUT => the 411 requirement it lists is untestable there. Separately, Cloudflare's edge may add/normalize Content-Length before the Worker sees it, so the 411-on-absent-length behavior may not even reproduce in production. Spec should either drop 411 from the Worker contract or require a raw-socket cloud fixture and confirm edge behavior.

Valid: required 'bun run test' lane never exercised Worker/D1 and no vitest config existed. Genuinely fixed by D-TESTS (spec:503-520): cloud-contract.yml made a REQUIRED check running test:cloud + committed vitest.config.js excludes tests/cloud/** from the default glob (R-CLOUDTEST).

Valid: string-matching one driver's constraint wording could map duplicate-id PUT to 500 on remote D1. VERIFIED CLOSED. Re-derived against D-STORE layers 1-3 (spec L230-251) + R-GUARD-3 (L576-581). Layer-1 pre-check 'ids.length !== new Set(ids).size' lives in the SHARED handler (server/handler.js, plan step 1) and runs BEFORE any DB call in BOTH adapters, so a duplicate id never reaches D1/bun:sqlite -> both runtimes return 400 deterministically with ZERO dependence on driver error wording. The mandated assets block pins run_worker_first: ["/api/*"] (a supported Wrangler v4 feature) so the Worker fetch handler runs FIRST for every /api/* request incl. unknown routes/methods, returning JSON 404/405 and never delegating /api to env.ASSETS.fetch; not_found_handling=single-page-application applies ONLY to non-/api paths. New approach expresses the cloud path via include (no positional filter), so the exclude-overrides-positional interaction is gone; globs resolve from config-file/project root, cwd-independent.
